Summary:
The paper presents a collaborative implicit neural simultaneous localization and mapping (SLAM) system for RGB-D image sequences, which is able to merge sub-maps into a global map and also handle loop-closure for neural mapper for the first time. To facilitate the capability of doing loop-closure and sub-map merging, the authors introduce a neural point-based 3D scene representation, where each point maintains a learnable neural feature for scene encoding and is associated with a keyframe. The optimization follows the traditional SLAM’s graph optimization fashion. The experimental results on diverse datasets demonstrate the superior performance of the proposed method in both camera tracking and mapping.

Strengths:
1. Novel Neural Point-based 3D Scene Representation: A neural point cloud representation for scene encoding in the proposed framework is a significant contribution. This approach allows each point to carry encoded information, improving the system's ability to represent and cross-view association for large-scale tracking and pose refinement. 
2.Comprehensive Collaborative Framework: The authors provide a collaborative SLAM system with front-end and back-end modules, addressing essential aspects such as odometry, loop detection, sub-map fusion, and global refinement. This comprehensive approach ensures that the proposed system can handle various challenges typically encountered in SLAM tasks. 
3.Distributed-to-Centralized Learning Strategy: The incorporation of a distributed-to-centralized learning strategy is an effective solution for improving consistency and cooperation in collaborative implicit SLAM. 
4.Improved Performance: The experimental results on multiple datasets demonstrate the superiority of the proposed method in both camera tracking and mapping.

Weaknesses:
1.In the Introduction: This paper emphasizes the significance of targeting collaborative-SLAM, considering the substantial value already provided by loop-closure capability in effectively reducing pose drift. However, the evaluation of the paper becomes more challenging due to the necessity for additional comparative studies that can convincingly demonstrate the advantages offered by both approaches.

2.In Section 2: For collaborative mapping, this paper should cite more recent works including:
[1*] Campos, Carlos, et al. "Orb-slam3: An accurate open-source library for visual, visual–inertial, and multimap slam." IEEE Transactions on Robotics 37.6 (2021): 1874-1890.
[2*] Tian, Yulun, et al. "Kimera-multi: Robust, distributed, dense metric-semantic slam for multi-robot systems." IEEE Transactions on Robotics 38.4 (2022).

It is also expected to provide comparative studies to the above researches.

3.In Section 3: 
(1). Line 101: This paper introduced 4x4 patches to compute the point features. However, it misses details on there is a limitation on the number of patches. What if we increase the size of the patches? Will it impact the later performance? 
(2) This paper introduced so many symbols but without giving any explanation of the symbols, which makes it really hard to follow the paper. Please revise the whole paper and make them clear to the readers.
(3) Equation 10: The explanation “Considering the strong non-convexity of the color map, only the geometry part is included in tracking loss” for not using color in the loss is not convincing. The authors are expected to provide ablation studies to prove the claim.

4.In Section 4: More experiments are expected:
(1). More comparative studies on Collaborative-SLAM are expected.
(2). For single-agent cases: The local covisibility map can be formed as well; it would be interesting to only look at cases without a loop but having a local covisibility map.
(3). Ablation study on “Distributed-to-Centralized Learning” is needed to prove why this is needed.

Questions:
1.In the context of this paper, which relies on point features for establishing frame-to-frame correspondence, an important concern arises regarding the maintenance of continuous tracking. Unlike the traditional SLAM pipeline, where feature locations typically reside around corners, this paper selects points without necessarily adhering to this characteristic. Thus, it becomes crucial to address how this approach ensures the continuity of tracking throughout the SLAM process.
2.Collaborative mapping can be done by recent work on No Pose Nerf:
[]Bian, Wenjing, et al. "Nope-nerf: Optimising neural radiance field with no pose prior." Proceedings of the IEEE/CVF Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition. 2023.
This paper should provide a comparison to show the advantage of the proposed method.
3. This paper has too many contributions, which does not form a scientific paper but more an engineering paper. Not sure if this paper fall into the NeuralIPS group.

Limitations:
1. The writing should be improved, especially the equations should be better explained.
2. More experiments and ablation studies should be provided to demonstrate the advantage of the proposed algorithms. 
3. The literature review needs to refer to more recent research.

Rating:
6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
reviews.text:
# Hybrid Peer Review: CP-SLAM - Collaborative Neural Point-based SLAM

## Summary

The paper presents CP-SLAM, a collaborative neural implicit SLAM system for RGB-D image sequences that combines traditional SLAM pipelines with neural implicit representations. The core contribution is a novel neural point-based 3D scene representation where each point maintains learnable neural features and is associated with a keyframe. This representation enables the system to perform loop closure and sub-map merging—capabilities previously unavailable in neural mappers. The system comprises comprehensive front-end (odometry, loop detection) and back-end (sub-map fusion, global refinement) modules. A distributed-to-centralized learning strategy using federated averaging facilitates multi-agent collaboration, while pose graph optimization and global map refinement improve accuracy. Experiments on Replica and TUM-RGBD datasets demonstrate state-of-the-art performance in both camera tracking and dense mapping.

## Strengths

1. **Novel Contribution Addressing a Real Gap**: This appears to be the first collaborative neural implicit SLAM system with complete front-end and back-end modules. The neural point-based representation is well-motivated as a meaningful improvement over voxel grid approaches, naturally enabling flexible map fusion and pose adjustment after loop closure—a capability that prior neural SLAM methods lacked.

2. **Well-Designed Technical Approach**: The keyframe-centric point representation is elegantly motivated (Figure 2 effectively illustrates advantages over voxel grids). The distributed-to-centralized learning strategy using federated averaging principles is a reasonable approach for multi-agent consistency. The integration of pose graph optimization with global map refinement properly combines traditional SLAM techniques with neural representations. Ablation studies (Section 4.4) systematically validate key components, demonstrating that pose graph optimization improves accuracy by 10-13% and showing the critical importance of sampling concentration.

3. **Comprehensive System Design**: The paper provides a complete SLAM pipeline addressing odometry, loop detection, sub-map fusion, and global refinement. This comprehensive approach ensures the system handles various challenges typically encountered in SLAM tasks, contrasting favorably with prior neural methods that addressed only subset of these capabilities.

4. **Strong Experimental Validation**: The paper includes both single-agent scenarios with loop closure (Table 2) and two-agent collaborative experiments (Table 1), showing consistent improvements over recent neural SLAM methods (NICE-SLAM, Vox-Fusion) and traditional approaches (ORB-SLAM3). The experiments include quantitative metrics (ATE RMSE, depth L1, mesh accuracy) and qualitative visualizations. Ablation studies validate design choices including point density and sampling concentration.

## Major Weaknesses

1. **Limited Scope of Real-World Collaborative Evaluation**: While the paper claims collaborative SLAM as a primary contribution, collaborative experiments are limited entirely to synthetic Replica datasets. Table 3 provides real-world validation only for single-agent scenarios on TUM-RGBD. This represents a critical gap: the paper lacks multi-agent experiments on real-world data with ground truth relative poses between agents. Without real-world collaborative validation, claims about the practical value of the collaborative component remain unsubstantiated. Additionally, evaluation is restricted to only two-agent scenarios; scalability to larger multi-agent systems (3+ agents) is completely unaddressed.

2. **Insufficient Justification and Analysis of Core Design Choices**: 
   - The distributed-to-centralized learning strategy, while novel, lacks theoretical justification. Why is federated averaging of MLPs optimal for SLAM? What are the convergence guarantees? The paper provides only a brief 3-paragraph description without formal specification of the averaging mechanism or fine-tuning procedure.
   - The claim in Equation 10 that color is excluded from tracking loss due to "strong non-convexity" is not convincing and requires ablation studies to validate. Why would non-convexity specifically argue against color inclusion?
   - The paper introduces many symbols without clear explanation (Section 3), making it difficult to follow the technical content. For example, Λ in Equation 12 appears without proper definition.

3. **Computational Requirements and Practical Applicability**: The system requires substantial GPU resources (RTX3090 per agent plus additional server GPU), with tracking time of 10.10s per frame (Table 6)—far slower than real-time requirements and significantly slower than Vox-Fusion (0.36-0.83s per frame). Memory footprint (0.62MB for features) is also higher than Vox-Fusion (0.15MB). While the authors acknowledge these limitations, they provide no solutions or analysis of computational bottlenecks. The practical deployment implications for resource-constrained robotic systems are concerning and inadequately addressed.

4. **Acknowledged Loop Closure Limitations Not Adequately Addressed**: The authors acknowledge that relative pose computation relies on rendering-based optimization, which may be inaccurate for large viewpoint changes, potentially causing map fusion drift. However, this limitation is neither quantified nor addressed. What is the maximum viewpoint angle the method can handle? How frequently do loop detection failures occur in practice? This is particularly concerning for real-world scenarios where significant viewpoint variations are common.

## Minor Weaknesses and Missing Analyses

1. **Incomplete Literature Review and Comparisons**: The paper should cite and compare against more recent collaborative SLAM works, including ORB-SLAM3 (Campos et al., 2021) and Kimera-Multi (Tian et al., 2022). Additionally, comparative studies with No-Pose NeRF (Bian et al., 2023) would be valuable for positioning the work relative to other pose-free approaches. The comparison with ORB-SLAM3 is somewhat unfair as ORB-SLAM3 is not designed for multi-sequence collaborative processing; the workaround of concatenating sequences may not represent its true collaborative capabilities. For two-agent experiments, CCM-SLAM fails on some scenes (marked with '-' in Table 1), making direct comparison difficult.

2. **Missing Ablation Studies and Sensitivity Analysis**: 
   - No ablation on the two-stage learning strategy itself. The paper doesn't compare distributed-only vs. centralized-only vs. hybrid approaches to justify why both stages are necessary.
   - The paper lacks analysis of loop detection threshold sensitivity. How sensitive is the system to λ_fine and λ_coarse? Were these tuned on validation data?
   - Limited ablation on hyperparameters: how was the query radius r=0.15m chosen? Is this scene-dependent? How sensitive is performance to the patch size (4x4) mentioned in Section 3?
   - No analysis of how many federated averaging iterations are required for convergence, or how convergence depends on network topology or agent count.

3. **Presentation and Technical Clarity Issues**:
   - Section 3 introduces numerous symbols without adequate explanation, significantly hindering readability. The paper should be revised to clarify all notation.
   - The federated learning mechanism and fine-tuning procedure lack implementation details. How many iterations constitute "lightweight fine-tuning"? On how many frames is this performed?
   - The keyframe association procedure for newly created points is not clearly specified.

4. **Incomplete Analysis of System Behavior**:
   - No discussion of failure modes or when the method might not work well (low-texture environments, high motion blur, rapid illumination changes, dynamic objects).
   - No analysis of communication overhead in collaborative scenarios, synchronization requirements, or robustness to communication delays/failures.
   - Table 3 shows performance degradation on real-world data compared to synthetic Replica results, but failure modes are not analyzed. What scene characteristics cause this degradation?
   - The paper does not address how the system handles dynamic scenes or what happens when loop detection produces false positives/negatives.

5. **Limited Generalization and Scope**:
   - All experiments are on indoor RGB-D sequences. No evaluation on outdoor scenes, monocular cameras, or other sensor modalities.
   - Only three TUM-RGBD sequences are evaluated (Table 3). Broader evaluation on diverse real-world datasets (ScanNet, 7-Scenes, ICL-NUIM) would strengthen applicability claims.
   - The acknowledged limitation regarding weaker hole-filling in unobserved regions compared to feature grids may be problematic for incomplete scene coverage but is not thoroughly analyzed.

## Questions for Authors

1. **Real-World Collaborative Validation**: Why are collaborative experiments limited to synthetic Replica data? Can you provide results on real-world multi-agent datasets with ground truth relative poses?

2. **Federated Learning Convergence**: How many rounds of federated averaging are performed before convergence? Does performance improve monotonically? What is the communication cost between agents and server?

3. **Loop Closure Robustness**: Can you quantify the accuracy degradation as a function of viewpoint angle? At what point does performance degrade significantly?

4. **Scalability Beyond Two Agents**: How does the system scale to

This comprehensive approach ensures that the proposed system can handle various challenges typically encountered in SLAM tasks. 
3.Distributed-to-Centralized Learning Strategy: The incorporation of a distributed-to-centralized learning strategy is an effective solution for improving consistency and cooperation in collaborative implicit SLAM.

1.In the Introduction: This paper emphasizes the significance of targeting collaborative-SLAM, considering the substantial value already provided by loop-closure capability in effectively reducing pose drift. 2.In Section 2: For collaborative mapping, this paper should cite more recent works including:

It is also expected to provide comparative studies to the above researches.

3.In Section 3: 
(1). Line 101: This paper introduced 4x4 patches to compute the point features. However, it misses details on there is a limitation on the number of patches. What if we increase the size of the patches? Will it impact the later performance? 
(2) This paper introduced so many symbols but without giving any explanation of the symbols, which makes it really hard to follow the paper. Please revise the whole paper and make them clear to the readers.

(1). More comparative studies on Collaborative-SLAM are expected.

This paper should provide a comparison to show the advantage of the proposed method.

reviews.summary: There have been some papers on getting dense Visual SLAM with neural fields published in the last couple of years. In this work, the authors proposed a set of additions to prior pipelines that get the NN SLAM closer to the traditional approaches concerning the overall framework. Specifically, the authors addressed loop detection, pose graph optimization, bundle adjustment, and collaborative SLAM.
reviews.strengths:
1) Visual-SLAM with neural fields has the huge benefit of providing a dense map of the environment. The topic is relevant to computer vision, robotics, and I believe it fits the NeurIPS scope.
2) The paper is well-written, easy to follow, and well-organized. Related work is OK.
3) Although the technical novelties are relatively limited, in my opinion, the contributions to the pipeline are significant.
reviews.weaknesses:
1) As written above, the technical contributions are limited. The paper focuses more on adding new steps to prior Visual SLAM pipelines.
2) The inclusion of the NetVLAD (not new) as a tool for loop detection looks like a trick. It is not elegant, in the sense that it is a parallel feature extractor that (maybe) could be done using the neural field encoders.
3) Only one dataset and 4 sequences are used, which is small concerning quantity and variety.
4) I would prefer to see more comparisons with previous traditional methods (without neural fields).
5) Figures 5 and 6 are not easily readable.
reviews.questions:
1) I would like the authors to explain what is the motivation for the inclusion of NetVLAD. Can't those descriptors be obtained from the neural field?
2) It is written that a pre-trained NetVLAD was used. It would be important to give more details about this training and how it generalizes to different scenes.
3) Usually, with classical V-SLAM, the bundle adjustment estimates both the pose of the cameras and the 3D map simultaneously. It seems that the authors are doing this alternatively. I would like to see some comments on this.
4) Why are the results for Apartment 2 so bad for the proposed approach? I would like the authors to comment on this.
5) It seems that traditional V-SLAM methods using RGB-D are missing (such as OrbSLAM). I would like to see some comments on this.
6) Maybe I missed something. But it is not clear to me how is the error in Tab. 2 computed.
reviews.limitations: Limitations are adequately addressed.
reviews.rating: 5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
There have been some papers on getting dense Visual SLAM with neural fields published in the last couple of years. In this work, the authors proposed a set of additions to prior pipelines that get the NN SLAM closer to the traditional approaches concerning the overall framework. Specifically, the authors addressed loop detection, pose graph optimization, bundle adjustment, and collaborative SLAM.

Strengths:
1) Visual-SLAM with neural fields has the huge benefit of providing a dense map of the environment. The topic is relevant to computer vision, robotics, and I believe it fits the NeurIPS scope.
2) The paper is well-written, easy to follow, and well-organized. Related work is OK.
3) Although the technical novelties are relatively limited, in my opinion, the contributions to the pipeline are significant.

Weaknesses:
1) As written above, the technical contributions are limited. The paper focuses more on adding new steps to prior Visual SLAM pipelines.
2) The inclusion of the NetVLAD (not new) as a tool for loop detection looks like a trick. It is not elegant, in the sense that it is a parallel feature extractor that (maybe) could be done using the neural field encoders.
3) Only one dataset and 4 sequences are used, which is small concerning quantity and variety.
4) I would prefer to see more comparisons with previous traditional methods (without neural fields).
5) Figures 5 and 6 are not easily readable.

Questions:
1) I would like the authors to explain what is the motivation for the inclusion of NetVLAD. Can't those descriptors be obtained from the neural field?
2) It is written that a pre-trained NetVLAD was used. It would be important to give more details about this training and how it generalizes to different scenes.
3) Usually, with classical V-SLAM, the bundle adjustment estimates both the pose of the cameras and the 3D map simultaneously. It seems that the authors are doing this alternatively. I would like to see some comments on this.
4) Why are the results for Apartment 2 so bad for the proposed approach? I would like the authors to comment on this.
5) It seems that traditional V-SLAM methods using RGB-D are missing (such as OrbSLAM). I would like to see some comments on this.
6) Maybe I missed something. But it is not clear to me how is the error in Tab. 2 computed.

Limitations:
Limitations are adequately addressed.

Rating:
5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.
reviews.text:
# Hybrid Peer Review: CP-SLAM

## Summary

This paper proposes CP-SLAM, a collaborative neural implicit SLAM system for RGB-D sequences that combines traditional SLAM pipelines with neural field representations. The core contribution is a neural point-based 3D scene representation where each point maintains learnable neural features and is associated with a keyframe, enabling flexible adjustment during pose optimization. The system incorporates a complete pipeline including odometry, loop detection via NetVLAD, sub-map fusion, and global refinement through pose graph optimization. A distributed-to-centralized learning strategy using federated averaging enables multi-agent collaboration. Experiments on Replica and TUM-RGBD datasets demonstrate improvements over recent neural SLAM methods and traditional approaches in both camera tracking and dense mapping.

## Strengths

1. **Relevance and Originality**: Visual SLAM with neural fields is a relevant topic for computer vision and robotics. This appears to be the first collaborative neural implicit SLAM system with complete front-end and back-end modules, addressing a genuine gap in the literature. The neural point-based representation is well-motivated as an improvement over voxel grids for enabling flexible map fusion and pose adjustment—Figure 2 effectively illustrates these advantages.

2. **Presentation Quality**: The paper is well-written, well-organized, and easy to follow. The method section systematically describes each component from odometry through global refinement. Figures 1-4 effectively communicate the system pipeline and key concepts, though some figures (particularly Figures 5-6) could benefit from improved readability.

3. **Comprehensive System Design**: The paper presents a complete SLAM pipeline integrating odometry, loop detection, sub-map fusion, and global optimization in a unified framework. The keyframe-centric point representation elegantly solves the pose adjustment problem after loop closure, which is a meaningful contribution to the pipeline architecture.

4. **Experimental Validation**: The paper includes extensive experiments on both single-agent scenarios with loop closure (Table 2) and two-agent collaborative scenarios (Table 1), with quantitative metrics (ATE RMSE, depth L1, mesh accuracy) and qualitative visualizations. Ablation studies systematically validate design choices, demonstrating that pose graph optimization improves accuracy by 10-13% and showing the criticality of sampling concentration.

5. **Technical Soundness**: The distributed-to-centralized learning strategy using federated learning principles is reasonable, and the integration of pose graph optimization with global map refinement is properly executed. The system shows consistent improvements over baselines including NICE-SLAM, Vox-Fusion, and ORB-SLAM3 across multiple metrics.

## Weaknesses

1. **Limited Technical Novelty**: While the contributions to the pipeline are significant, the technical novelties are relatively limited. The paper primarily focuses on adding established components to prior Visual SLAM pipelines rather than introducing fundamentally new techniques. The loop detection mechanism using NetVLAD is particularly concerning in this regard—it appears to be a parallel feature extractor that could potentially be derived from the neural field encoders themselves. The authors should explain the motivation for this choice: Why can't loop closure descriptors be obtained directly from the neural field representation? This design decision lacks elegance and feels like a workaround rather than an integrated solution.

2. **Insufficient Experimental Scope**: The evaluation is restricted to synthetic Replica datasets for primary experiments, with limited real-world validation on TUM-RGBD using only three sequences. Critically, all collaborative experiments are conducted on synthetic data only—no real-world multi-agent validation exists, which significantly undermines claims about practical applicability of the collaborative component. The paper lacks evaluation on diverse real-world environments, especially large-scale outdoor scenes, and demonstrates only two-agent scenarios despite claiming collaborative capability. Scalability to more than two agents remains entirely unexplored.

3. **Computational Requirements and Efficiency**: The system requires substantial GPU resources (RTX3090 per agent plus an additional server GPU), limiting practical deployment in resource-constrained robotic systems. Table 6 shows tracking time of 10.10s per frame, which is far from real-time and significantly slower than Vox-Fusion (0.83s for tracking). Memory footprint is also higher than alternatives (0.62MB vs. 0.15MB for Vox-Fusion). The paper acknowledges these limitations but provides no solutions or analysis of computational bottlenecks. The scalability of federated averaging with increasing numbers of agents is not analyzed.

4. **Loop Closure and Pose Graph Optimization Concerns**: 
   - The pre-trained NetVLAD is used without scene-specific adaptation. The paper lacks details about this training and how well it generalizes to different scenes. Sensitivity to loop detection failures is not analyzed—what happens when NetVLAD produces false positives or negatives?
   - The authors acknowledge that relative pose computation relies on rendering-based optimization, which may be inaccurate for large viewpoint changes, potentially causing map fusion drift. This is a significant limitation for real-world scenarios with substantial viewpoint variations, but no quantitative analysis of failure modes or robustness thresholds is provided.
   - Unlike classical V-SLAM methods that estimate camera poses and 3D maps simultaneously during bundle adjustment, this work performs these optimizations alternately. The implications of this design choice warrant more detailed discussion.

5. **Incomplete Comparisons and Methodological Issues**:
   - For two-agent experiments, CCM-SLAM fails on some scenes (marked with '-' in Table 1), making direct comparison difficult. The comparison with ORB-SLAM3 is somewhat unfair, as ORB-SLAM3 is not designed for true collaborative processing; the workaround of concatenating sequences may not fairly represent its capabilities.
   - Traditional RGB-D SLAM methods (such as ORB-SLAM3) appear to be underrepresented in comparisons. More comprehensive evaluation against traditional approaches would strengthen the paper.
   - The paper lacks ablation studies on the two-stage learning strategy itself. No comparison between distributed-only, centralized-only, and hybrid approaches is provided. How many iterations of federated averaging are needed for convergence? Does performance improve monotonically with more rounds?

6. **Underspecified Technical Details**: 
   - The distributed-to-centralized learning strategy (Section 3.3) is described very briefly and lacks implementation details. The federated averaging mechanism and fine-tuning procedure could be more formally specified. What constitutes "lightweight fine-tuning," and how many iterations are used?
   - The error computation in Table 2 is not clearly explained.
   - Communication overhead and synchronization requirements for multi-agent collaboration are not discussed.
   - The choice of hyperparameters (e.g., query radius r=0.15m, ρ=0.14m) appears empirically driven with limited sensitivity analysis. How scene-dependent are these parameters?

7. **Unexplained Performance Anomalies**: The results for Apartment 2 in the proposed approach appear notably worse than other sequences. The authors should provide explicit commentary on this failure case and what scene characteristics cause degradation.

8. **Limited Discussion of Failure Modes**: The paper does not adequately discuss when the method might not work well—e.g., in low-texture environments, with high motion blur, rapid illumination changes, or dynamic scenes. The keyframe-centric representation may create issues when keyframe association becomes ambiguous.

## Questions for Authors

1. **NetVLAD Justification**: What is the motivation for including NetVLAD as a parallel feature extractor? Why can't loop closure descriptors be obtained directly from the neural field encoders? How does the pre-trained NetVLAD generalize to different scenes, and how sensitive is the system to the fixed thresholds λ_fine and λ_coarse?

2. **Bundle Adjustment Strategy**: Classical V-SLAM methods estimate poses and maps simultaneously. Your approach performs these optimizations alternately. What are the implications of this design choice compared to simultaneous optimization?

3. **Scalability Analysis**: How does the system scale beyond two agents? What are the computational and communication bottlenecks for 4, 8, or more agents? How many federated averaging iterations are performed, and does convergence depend on network topology or agent count?

4. **Loop Closure Robustness**: Can you quantify the failure modes for rendering-based pose optimization? At what viewpoint angle does performance degrade significantly? How often does loop detection fail in practice?

5. **Real-World Collaborative Validation**: Why are collaborative experiments limited to synthetic Replica data? Can you provide results on real-world multi-agent datasets with ground truth relative poses?

6. **Apartment 2 Performance**: Why does the proposed approach perform poorly on Apartment 2 compared to other sequences? What scene characteristics cause this degradation?

7. **Error Computation**: How is the error in Table 2 computed? Please provide explicit formulation.

8. **Hyperparameter Sensitivity**: How sensitive is the system to query radius r and point density ρ? Were these tuned on
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
There have been some papers on getting dense Visual SLAM with neural fields published in the last couple of years. In this work, the authors proposed a set of additions to prior pipelines that get the NN SLAM closer to the traditional approaches concerning the overall framework. Specifically, the authors addressed loop detection, pose graph optimization, bundle adjustment, and collaborative SLAM.

Strengths:
1) Visual-SLAM with neural fields has the huge benefit of providing a dense map of the environment. The topic is relevant to computer vision, robotics, and I believe it fits the NeurIPS scope.
2) The paper is well-written, easy to follow, and well-organized. Related work is OK.
3) Although the technical novelties are relatively limited, in my opinion, the contributions to the pipeline are significant.

Weaknesses:
1) As written above, the technical contributions are limited. The paper focuses more on adding new steps to prior Visual SLAM pipelines.
2) The inclusion of the NetVLAD (not new) as a tool for loop detection looks like a trick. It is not elegant, in the sense that it is a parallel feature extractor that (maybe) could be done using the neural field encoders.
3) Only one dataset and 4 sequences are used, which is small concerning quantity and variety.
4) I would prefer to see more comparisons with previous traditional methods (without neural fields).
5) Figures 5 and 6 are not easily readable.

Questions:
1) I would like the authors to explain what is the motivation for the inclusion of NetVLAD. Can't those descriptors be obtained from the neural field?
2) It is written that a pre-trained NetVLAD was used. It would be important to give more details about this training and how it generalizes to different scenes.
3) Usually, with classical V-SLAM, the bundle adjustment estimates both the pose of the cameras and the 3D map simultaneously. It seems that the authors are doing this alternatively. I would like to see some comments on this.
4) Why are the results for Apartment 2 so bad for the proposed approach? I would like the authors to comment on this.
5) It seems that traditional V-SLAM methods using RGB-D are missing (such as OrbSLAM). I would like to see some comments on this.
6) Maybe I missed something. But it is not clear to me how is the error in Tab. 2 computed.

Limitations:
Limitations are adequately addressed.

Rating:
5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.

title: Learning Repeatable Speech Embeddings Using An Intra-class Correlation Regularizer

reviews.summary: Starting with the observation that supervised embeddings should vary from one class to another but not be sensitive to variations within a given class, this paper proposes to add an intra-class correlation (ICC) regularization to the contrastive loss in representation learning. This new regularizer is derived from measurement theory and encourages the embeddings to have a low intra-class variance, whereas contrastive losses pushes more the embeddings towards high inter-class variances. After a simulation illustrating the "behaviour" of these losses with respect to inter- and intra-class variance, the authors report good improvements using the proposed ICC regularizer for three different tasks.
reviews.strengths:
- The paper is overall well written, the idea well explained and easy to follow and to understand
  - The ICC regularizer seems to really focus on intra-class variance, i.e. make all occurences of the same class have the same embeddings, where contrastive loss does both at the same time and never really focuses on minimizing the intra-class variance.
  - The method is relatively simple, very well explained and easy to understand. The simulation, comparison with contrastive loss and classification error rate is quite interesting.
  - When the embeddings are constrained, it is indeed useful to focus on minimizing the intra-class variance to separate classes. The explanation and results are convincing.
reviews.weaknesses:
- Missing comparison with other techniques mentioned in the related work (data augmentation, etc.). Is the proposed approach competitive with these methods? Could it be complementary? How does it compare to VICReg [4] for example
  - In the result section, the text mainly lists the results in the tables. Maybe a reformulation would allow more space for example to study the effect of the regularization weight
reviews.questions:
Could that method apply to self-supervised approaches, where the notion of classes is less well defined?

Is it competitive with other methods mentioned at the beginning of the paper? Or could it be complementary?

Are the improvements with that method bigger on smaller datasets or are they independent of the dataset size? Is the method especially suited for some tasks and less for other (e.g. could that be useful for classification, or ASR?)?

minor remark:
  - sec 3.1: "represents the i-th samples" -> sample
reviews.limitations: yes

# Hybrid Peer Review

## Summary

This paper proposes using the intra-class correlation coefficient (ICC) from measurement theory to improve the repeatability of learned embeddings in representation learning. The authors introduce an ICC regularizer as a complementary component to contrastive losses, designed to explicitly minimize intra-class variance while maintaining inter-class separation. The method is motivated by the observation that supervised embeddings should vary between classes but remain consistent within a class. The authors provide theoretical intuition via Monte Carlo simulation comparing their approach to existing contrastive losses (GE2E, AngleProto, SupCon) and validate the method on three speech tasks: text-independent speaker verification (~10% EER improvement), zero-shot voice style conversion (62% preference in AB tests), and dysphonic voice detection (30.68% ICC improvement). Code is made publicly available.

## Strengths

The paper presents several notable strengths. First, the conceptual connection between measurement theory's repeatability and representation learning is novel and well-motivated. The core insight—that ICC regularization focuses specifically on minimizing intra-class variance while contrastive losses simultaneously optimize both inter- and intra-class objectives—is clearly articulated and supported by the simulation analysis in Section 3.2. The Monte Carlo simulation effectively illustrates why the ICC regularizer differs from GE2E loss through contour analysis and gradient derivations (Equations 7-8), providing valuable intuition for why decoupling these objectives is beneficial.

The paper is overall well-written with clear exposition of the method. The ICC regularizer itself is relatively simple and easy to understand, and the mathematical formulation (Equations 2-6) is sound. The simulation comparing ICC regularizer to contrastive loss and classification error rate is particularly insightful, demonstrating that when embeddings are constrained, focusing on minimizing intra-class variance can effectively separate classes.

The experimental validation is comprehensive across three diverse speech tasks, demonstrating consistent improvements across multiple contrastive losses (GE2E, AngleProto, SupCon) and different architectures (VGG-M-40, FastResNet-34). The improvements are consistent across domains, suggesting the method has some generalizability within the speech processing domain. Additionally, the authors demonstrate reproducibility through open-sourced code and clear documentation of hyperparameters, datasets, and training details. The paper also provides an honest discussion of limitations, acknowledging that ICC is computed per-dimension rather than holistically and explaining why it requires complementary contrastive losses rather than functioning as a standalone objective.

## Weaknesses

Despite these strengths, several important limitations merit discussion. While the simulation provides useful intuition, there is limited theoretical justification for why ICC specifically is the optimal metric for the stated goal. ICC was designed for inter-rater reliability in measurement theory; the connection to embedding quality could be more rigorously established. The paper would benefit from comparison to other metrics that also measure intra/inter-class variance (e.g., silhouette coefficient, Davies-Bouldin index, Calinski-Harabasz index) to justify the choice of ICC over alternatives. Furthermore, the simulation uses only 8-dimensional Gaussian mixtures; generalization to high-dimensional real embeddings (e.g., 256D) and different class counts remains unclear.

The core novelty is somewhat limited—applying an existing statistical metric as a regularizer, while useful, is relatively straightforward. The ICC regularizer is presented as complementary to contrastive loss rather than as a standalone contribution, which constrains its conceptual impact. Importantly, the paper acknowledges but does not deeply explore why ICC cannot replace contrastive loss; understanding this fundamental limitation would strengthen the theoretical contribution.

The method requires task-specific tuning of the hyperparameter λ (ranging from 0.06 to 0.25 across experiments), with limited guidance for practitioners. While Figure B.1 shows linear scaling of λ, no systematic sensitivity analysis or principled selection method is provided. The paper lacks principled heuristics for selecting λ without extensive grid search, which limits practical applicability.

A critical constraint is that the ICC regularizer requires balanced batches with equal class sizes (M samples per class). While Appendix A addresses imbalanced classes with an extended formulation, this constraint is insufficiently emphasized in the main paper and the extended formulation is not experimentally validated despite being claimed in the GitHub repository. The paper would benefit from empirical evaluation on naturally imbalanced datasets to demonstrate the effectiveness of this extension. Additionally, the two-branch architecture required for Task 3 (dysphonic voice detection) to avoid convergence failures suggests potential fundamental incompatibilities between the ICC regularizer and certain objectives—this architectural dependency deserves deeper theoretical analysis rather than being presented as a minor implementation detail.

A significant gap is the absence of comparisons with other techniques mentioned in the related work. The paper does not compare against recent methods addressing intra-class variance minimization, such as VICReg [4], which is cited but never empirically compared. Task 1 compares only to contrastive losses, and Task 3 uses older baselines (2017-2021) without comparing to more recent dysphonic detection methods. The paper would be substantially strengthened by demonstrating whether the proposed approach is competitive with, complementary to, or superior to these existing methods.

The experimental evaluation, while comprehensive in scope, has several methodological gaps. Task 1 reports point estimates and minDCF but provides no confidence intervals or significance tests. Task 2's AB preference test uses only 18 listeners and 100 pairs, which is a relatively limited sample size; inter-rater agreement metrics (e.g., Fleiss' kappa) are not reported. While Task 3 provides confidence intervals, formal hypothesis testing is absent for Tasks 1-2. Additionally, in Table 4, the method underperforms on SVD training accuracy (0.7353 vs 0.8003 for Zhang et al.) despite higher ICC scores, suggesting potential misalignment between ICC optimization and downstream task performance—this discrepancy deserves investigation.

The scope is limited to speech processing. Claims about generalizability to vision and NLP (Section 5) are currently unsupported. The paper would be significantly strengthened by preliminary results on non-speech domains (e.g., face recognition, person re-identification in vision; or embedding tasks in NLP) to validate broader applicability. Finally, the paper lacks discussion of computational overhead from ICC computation during training, particularly as embedding dimension L increases.

## Questions for Authors

1. **Metric justification**: Why is ICC superior to other intra/inter-class variance metrics (silhouette coefficient, Davies-Bouldin index, Calinski-Harabasz index)? Can you provide empirical comparisons or theoretical justification? Have you considered alternative ICC variants (ICC(2,1), ICC(3,1), Cronbach's alpha)?

2. **Hyperparameter selection**: Is there a principled method to select λ without grid search? Does λ correlate with dataset properties (class imbalance, embedding dimension, number of samples)? Can you provide guidance for practitioners selecting λ for new tasks?

3. **Batch dependency and imbalance**: How does performance degrade with imbalanced batches? Can you quantify the penalty when class sizes deviate from M? Can you experimentally validate the extended formulation (Appendix A) on naturally imbalanced datasets?

4. **Missing comparisons**: Why were VICReg and other recent intra-class variance minimization methods not compared in Task 1? Can you add these comparisons?

5. **Task 3 architecture**: Why did one- and two-embedding architectures fail to converge for Task 3? This suggests fundamental conflicts between ICC regularizer and certain objectives—can you provide theoretical analysis of this fragility?

6. **Generalization beyond speech**: Can you provide preliminary results on vision (e.g., face recognition, person re-identification) or NLP tasks to support claims about broader applicability?

7. **Statistical rigor**: For Tasks 1-2, can you provide confidence intervals and significance tests (e.g., paired t-tests)? For Task 2, can you report inter-rater agreement metrics (Fleiss' kappa)?

8. **Simulation generalization**: How do simulation results change with higher dimensionality (e.g., 256D) or different class counts beyond the 8D, 4-class setting?

9. **Causal relationship**: Can you provide ablation studies isolating the contribution of ICC regularization versus simply using better hyperparameter tuning of the contrastive loss alone?

10. **Computational cost**: What is the computational overhead of computing ICC during training relative to the contrastive loss? How does this scale with embedding dimension L and batch size?

11. **Societal impact**: For the clinical application (Task 3), can you provide empirical analysis of potential bias amplification and concrete mitigation strategies? What is the clinical significance of the 3% accuracy improvement in terms of false positive/negative rates?

## Minor Issues

- Section 3.1: "represents the i-th samples" should be "sample"
- The notation in Equations 1-4 is

reviews.text:
# Hybrid Peer Review

## Summary

This paper proposes using the intra-class correlation coefficient (ICC) from measurement theory to evaluate and improve the repeatability of deep-learned embeddings. The authors introduce an ICC regularizer as a complementary component to contrastive losses, designed to minimize intra-class variance while maintaining inter-class separation. The method is evaluated on three speech tasks: text-independent speaker verification (achieving ~10% EER improvement), zero-shot voice style conversion (62% preference in AB tests), and dysphonic voice detection (30.68% ICC improvement). The effectiveness of the approach is confirmed through comprehensive experimental validation, with code made publicly available for reproducibility.

## Strengths

The paper presents a well-motivated and conceptually novel perspective by connecting measurement theory's repeatability concept to representation learning. This is particularly insightful for speech applications where consistency across conditions is critical. The mathematical formulation is clear, with the ICC regularizer well-defined in Equations 2-6.

The theoretical motivation is strengthened by the Monte Carlo simulation in Section 3.2, which effectively illustrates why the ICC regularizer differs from the GE2E loss through contour analysis and gradient derivations (Equations 7-8). The observation that ICC focuses specifically on minimizing intra-class variance while GE2E simultaneously maximizes inter-class variance is genuinely insightful and well-illustrated in Figure 1. This simulation-based comparison provides intuitive understanding of the method's mechanics and clearly demonstrates the distinction between the proposed regularizer and existing contrastive losses.

The experimental validation is comprehensive, demonstrating the method's consistency across three diverse speech tasks and multiple contrastive loss functions (GE2E, AngleProto, SupCon) with different architectures (VGG-M-40, FastResNet-34). The improvements are consistent across all tested scenarios, which strengthens the claims of practical utility. The authors have made commendable efforts toward reproducibility by open-sourcing the code and providing detailed hyperparameters, datasets, and training procedures. Additionally, the paper demonstrates intellectual honesty by acknowledging that ICC operates dimension-wise rather than holistically and explaining why it requires complementary contrastive losses.

## Weaknesses

While the paper is technically sound, several concerns warrant discussion. First, the novelty is somewhat limited in scope—applying an existing statistical metric (ICC) as a regularizer, while useful, represents a relatively straightforward methodological contribution. The core insight that minimizing intra-class variance improves repeatability, though valuable, is intuitive rather than surprising. More fundamentally, the paper lacks rigorous theoretical justification for why ICC specifically is the optimal metric for this purpose. The authors do not compare ICC against other intra/inter-class variance metrics (e.g., silhouette coefficient, Davies-Bouldin index, Calinski-Harabasz index) that could similarly measure embedding quality. Why ICC, borrowed directly from measurement theory with minimal mathematical innovation, is superior to alternatives remains unclear.

The simulation analysis, while helpful, is conducted on synthetic 8-dimensional Gaussian mixtures with only 4 classes. Generalization to higher-dimensional real embeddings (e.g., 256D) and different class structures is unclear. This limitation is particularly important given that the method's behavior in high-dimensional spaces may differ substantially from the controlled simulation environment.

A practical limitation is the method's requirement for balanced class sizes in batches (M samples per class). While the authors provide an extended formulation for imbalanced classes in Appendix A, this extension is not experimentally validated despite being claimed in the GitHub repository. This is a significant gap, as real-world applications frequently exhibit severe class imbalance. The core method's applicability is therefore constrained, and practitioners lack empirical evidence for the extended formulation's effectiveness.

The hyperparameter selection process presents another concern. The method requires task-specific tuning of λ, with values ranging from 0.06 to 0.25 across experiments. The paper relies on grid search without providing systematic guidance for practitioners or principled heuristics for selecting λ on new tasks. Figure B.1 shows linear scaling but offers limited practical direction. Without a principled selection method, the reproducibility and generalizability of the approach are compromised.

The per-dimension independence assumption in ICC calculation (Eq. 5) may not capture global embedding geometry and could allow correlated dimensions to develop—a limitation the authors acknowledge but do not thoroughly investigate empirically. Quantifying whether and how this affects downstream performance would strengthen the analysis.

Additionally, the experimental improvements, while consistent, are modest in some cases. For Task 1, EER improvements vary significantly across loss functions (3.3%-10%), yet the paper does not explain this variation. For Task 2, the speaker similarity score actually decreases with ICC regularization (0.5109 vs 0.5810), which appears contradictory and deserves clarification. Task 3 reports a 3% accuracy improvement, but the clinical significance of this gain for real-world deployment is not discussed.

The convergence issues encountered in Task 3 with single- and dual-embedding architectures are particularly concerning. The fact that a two-branch architecture was necessary to avoid convergence failures suggests potential fundamental conflicts between the ICC regularizer and certain contrastive objectives. This architectural dependency is mentioned but not thoroughly analyzed, raising questions about the method's robustness and generalizability.

## Experimental and Methodological Concerns

Several issues regarding statistical rigor and baseline comparisons merit attention. Task 1 reports point estimates and minDCF but provides no confidence intervals or significance tests. Task 2's AB test uses only 18 listeners and 100 pairs, with no inter-rater agreement metrics (e.g., Fleiss' kappa) reported. While Task 3 provides confidence intervals, formal hypothesis testing is absent across all three tasks. The paper would benefit from paired t-tests or similar statistical significance tests comparing baselines with and without ICC regularization.

Baseline comparisons are incomplete. Task 1 compares only to contrastive losses; recent methods addressing intra-class variance (e.g., VICREG, cited but not compared) should be included. Task 3 compares to older baselines (2017-2021), missing more recent dysphonic detection methods. No comparison to other regularizers that minimize intra-class variance is provided. These omissions limit the assessment of the method's relative merit.

The paper lacks crucial ablation studies. No analysis is provided of how ICC performance scales with embedding dimension L, batch size N, or samples per class M. The sensitivity of results to batch composition is not quantified. The contribution of the ICC regularizer versus simply using better hyperparameter tuning of the contrastive loss alone is not isolated. Additionally, the computational overhead of ICC calculation during training—particularly with large embedding dimensions—is not discussed, making practical implementation guidance incomplete.

## Scope and Generalization

All three experimental tasks are speech-specific. While the authors mention generalizability to vision and NLP domains in Section 5, this is presented as future work with no preliminary evidence. Claims about broad applicability are therefore speculative and unsupported. The scope of the contribution would be significantly strengthened by even preliminary results on non-speech domains.

The societal impact discussion is generic and underdeveloped. While bias amplification is briefly mentioned, no empirical analysis or concrete mitigation strategies are proposed. Given the clinical application (dysphonia detection), where fairness and bias are critical, this discussion should be more thorough. The paper would benefit from bias evaluation across demographic groups and explicit protocols for mitigating potential harms in high-stakes clinical settings.

## Questions for Authors

1. Why is ICC (from measurement theory) superior to other intra/inter-class variance metrics for this task? Can you provide empirical comparisons or stronger theoretical justification?

2. Is there a principled method to select λ without grid search? Does λ correlate with dataset properties (class imbalance, embedding dimension, number of samples)?

3. How does performance degrade with imbalanced batches? Can you quantify the penalty when class sizes deviate from M? Is the extended formulation (Appendix A) as effective as balanced batches?

4. Why did the one- and two-embedding architectures fail to converge for Task 3? This suggests fundamental conflicts—can you provide theoretical analysis?

5. Why were recent methods like VICREG not compared in Task 1? Can you add comparisons to other intra-class variance minimization methods?

6. For Task 2, why is speaker similarity score lower with ICC regularization (0.5109 vs 0.5810) while other metrics improve? How do you explain this apparent contradiction?

7. In Table 4, why does your method underperform on SVD training accuracy (0.7353 vs 0.8003) despite higher ICC scores? Does this suggest ICC and task performance can be misaligned?

8. What is the computational overhead of computing ICC during training relative to the contrastive loss alone? Does this scale with embedding dimension L?

9. Can you provide statistical significance tests and confidence intervals for Tasks 1-2 similar to Task 3?

10. Have you evaluated the method on naturally imbal
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
The paper presents a simple but seemingly effective idea to regularize contrastive embedding extractors. The regularizer aims at the intra-class correlation to ensure the repeatability of the embedding.
The effectiveness is confirmed experimentally for the task of speaker verification, voice style conversion, and dysphonic voice detection.

Strengths:
The presented idea is well introduced and the illustration of the ICC regularizer action compared to the GE2E loss is insightful. The evaluation is sufficient.

Weaknesses:
The introduced regularizer uses 2nd order statistics only which could be a limiting factor on the embedding extractors but on the other hand this doesn't need to be required.

Questions:
The contrastive loss has a direct relationship to the mutual information, one could think that ICC is automatically included in the mutual information when only 2nd order statistics are taken into account, what could be the reason that this doesn't seem to be the case.

Limitations:
The introduced regularizer uses 2nd order statistics only.

Rating:
6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.

Summary:
In this paper, authors propose to use an intra-class correlation coefficient (ICC) regularizer as a complimentary component for contrastive loss to increase the repeatability of the supervised speech embedding. Authors conduct simulation for ICC regularizer and contrastive loss (GE2E loss) to explain their similarities and differences in the optimization criteria. Based on the experimental results on the speaker verification, voice style conversion, and a clinical application for detecting dysphonic three tasks, the authors claim that adding an ICC regularizer helps increase the repeatability and further improve the performance on downstream tasks.

Strengths:
1. This paper leverages the concept of repeatability and states that higher repeatability can contribute to improved performance in downstream tasks. Authors think about the importance of intra-class variance and the proposed ICC regularizer places greater emphasis on minimizing it to help increase repeatability. The simulation results in Section 3.2 explains the intuition clearly. 
 
2. As a complementary component to contrastive loss, adding the proposed ICC regularizer successfully increase the repeatability and shows good performance in the three different tasks.

Weaknesses:
1. In Task 1, the performance of SV system is not good though adding the ICC regularizer improves its performance. In this paper, VGG-M-40 is trained on VoxCeleb1 & 2. The best result (3.78% EER) reported in Table 1 is not as good as other commonly used models for SV. In Chung et al. paper, the VGG-M-40 shows much worse performance than Thin-ResNet34 and Fast ResNet34. To make the experimental solid and results and claims more convincible, authors should pick a good baseline instead of use the VGG-M-40.     

2. For better replicable, more experiment details should be provided, such as the training details of Task 1.     

3. To have comprehensive evaluation results for Task 1, the evaluation metric minDCF can be used as well.

4. Visualization (such as t-SNE) can be added as a comparison to see if the extracted speech or speaker embeddings have smaller intra-class variance in the space, though their ICC is decreased.

Questions:
1. As this paper mentioned, the ICC regularizer requires hyperparameter fine-tuning. Are the hyperparameter tuned on a validation set or a development set? 

2. As this paper mentioned in Section 3.2, though the ICC increased, do the learned embeddings are more compacted within-class and more separate between-class in the overall embedding space? Authors can use t-SNE to visualize them.

Limitations:
The authors do not analyze the potential negative societal impact.

# Hybrid Peer Review

## Summary

This paper proposes using the intra-class correlation coefficient (ICC) from measurement theory to evaluate and improve the repeatability of learned speech embeddings. The authors introduce an ICC regularizer as a complementary component to contrastive losses, designed to minimize intra-class variance while maintaining inter-class separation. The key contribution is connecting measurement theory's concept of repeatability to representation learning through a well-motivated regularization approach. The method is evaluated on three speech tasks: text-independent speaker verification (achieving ~10% EER improvement), zero-shot voice style conversion (62% preference in AB tests), and dysphonic voice detection (30.68% ICC improvement). The authors provide theoretical intuition via Monte Carlo simulation explaining why the ICC regularizer better focuses on reducing intra-class variance compared to contrastive losses alone, and they make code publicly available for reproducibility.

## Strengths

1. **Novel and well-motivated perspective**: The paper makes a creative connection between repeatability from measurement theory and deep learning representation quality. This is particularly well-justified for speech applications where consistency across conditions is critical. The conceptual link between ICC and embedding quality is clearly articulated through both theoretical analysis and empirical demonstration.

2. **Clear theoretical motivation with insightful analysis**: The Monte Carlo simulation in Section 3.2 effectively illustrates why the ICC regularizer differs from GE2E loss through contour analysis and gradient derivations (Equations 7-8). The observation that ICC focuses on minimizing intra-class variance while GE2E simultaneously maximizes inter-class variance is genuinely insightful and well-explained. The simulation provides valuable intuition, even if conducted on synthetic data.

3. **Comprehensive experimental validation**: The evaluation across three diverse speech tasks demonstrates broad applicability. Importantly, improvements are consistent across multiple contrastive losses (GE2E, AngleProto, SupCon) and architectures (VGG-M-40, FastResNet-34), strengthening claims about the method's generality. The fact that the ICC regularizer acts as a complementary component rather than a standalone loss makes it more practical and broadly applicable.

4. **Strong practical reproducibility**: The authors provide open-source code, clearly document hyperparameters, datasets, and training details, and honestly acknowledge limitations including the dimension-wise nature of ICC computation and why it requires complementary losses.

## Weaknesses

1. **Baseline architecture limitations and incomplete experimental scope**: While the paper demonstrates consistent improvements, the choice of VGG-M-40 for Task 1 is problematic. With a best result of 3.78% EER on VoxCeleb1&2, this baseline significantly underperforms more modern architectures. As noted in prior work (Chung et al.), VGG-M-40 shows substantially worse performance than Thin-ResNet34 and Fast ResNet34. To make the experimental validation more convincing and claims more credible, the authors should evaluate on stronger baseline models. Additionally, for Task 1, the evaluation could be more comprehensive by reporting minDCF alongside EER, and for Task 3, comparisons to more recent dysphonic detection methods (beyond 2017-2021 baselines) would strengthen the work.

2. **Hyperparameter sensitivity and selection methodology**: The method requires task-specific tuning of λ, with values ranging from 0.06 to 0.25 across experiments. While Figure B.1 shows linear scaling, the paper provides limited guidance for practitioners selecting λ for new tasks. Beyond grid search, no principled selection method or systematic sensitivity analysis is provided. This adds practical complexity and raises questions about how practitioners should approach hyperparameter selection without extensive tuning.

3. **Limited theoretical justification for metric choice**: While the simulation provides useful intuition, there is insufficient formal analysis of why ICC (Equation 2) is the optimal metric for the stated goal. ICC is designed for inter-rater reliability in measurement theory; the connection to embedding quality could be more rigorously justified. The paper does not compare ICC to other metrics that also measure intra/inter-class variance properties (e.g., silhouette coefficient, Davies-Bouldin index, Calinski-Harabasz index, or other recent intra-class variance minimization methods like VICREG). Why is ICC specifically superior to these alternatives?

4. **Batch dependency and class imbalance limitations**: The core method requires balanced batches with equal samples per class (M samples per class). While Appendix A addresses imbalanced classes, this constraint is not sufficiently emphasized in the main paper and significantly limits applicability to real-world scenarios with severe class imbalance. Critically, the extended formulation for imbalanced classes is not experimentally validated despite being claimed in the repository, leaving uncertainty about its practical effectiveness.

5. **Task-specific architectural requirements suggest potential fragility**: In Task 3 (dysphonic detection), a two-branch architecture was necessary to avoid convergence failures with simpler designs. This architectural dependency is concerning and suggests potential fundamental conflicts between the ICC regularizer and certain contrastive objectives. The paper does not provide theoretical analysis of why these convergence issues occur or when practitioners should expect similar problems. This raises questions about the method's generalizability and robustness.

6. **Insufficient reproducibility details and statistical rigor**: For Task 1, critical training details are missing (learning rate, optimization algorithm, convergence criteria, etc.). Task 1 reports point estimates and minDCF but no confidence intervals or significance tests. Task 2's AB preference test uses only 18 listeners and 100 pairs; inter-rater agreement metrics (e.g., Fleiss' kappa) are not reported, making it difficult to assess reliability. While Task 3 provides confidence intervals, formal hypothesis testing across all tasks would strengthen claims.

7. **Unexplained performance discrepancies**: In Table 4, the method underperforms Zhang et al. on SVD training accuracy (0.7353 vs 0.8003) despite higher ICC scores. This raises questions about the alignment between ICC optimization and downstream task performance. Similarly, in Task 2, speaker similarity scores are lower with ICC regularization (0.5109 vs 0.5810) while other metrics improve—these trade-offs deserve deeper investigation.

8. **Limited scope and unsubstantiated generalization claims**: All three tasks are speech-specific. Claims about generalizability to vision and NLP domains (Section 5) are mentioned as future work but remain entirely unsupported. The assertion that ICC is "rarely explicitly considered during training" across domains is not thoroughly validated.

9. **Incomplete negative societal impact analysis**: While the paper mentions potential bias amplification in clinical applications, the discussion is generic and underdeveloped. Given the clinical application in Task 3, more concrete analysis is needed: What are specific bias risks in dysphonia detection? How might ICC regularization amplify these risks? What mitigation strategies should be employed? For Task 3 specifically, the clinical significance of a 3% accuracy improvement is unclear—does this translate to meaningful changes in false positive/negative rates that matter for clinical decision-making?

10. **Unexplored computational considerations**: The paper does not discuss the computational overhead of ICC calculation during training, particularly as embedding dimension L increases. How does this scale relative to the contrastive loss alone?

## Questions and Suggestions

1. **Metric justification**: Beyond the simulation comparison, can you provide empirical comparisons of ICC to other intra/inter-class variance metrics (silhouette coefficient, Davies-Bouldin index) or recent methods like VICREG? What makes ICC theoretically superior for this application?

2. **Hyperparameter guidance**: Is there a principled method to select λ without grid search? Does λ correlate with measurable dataset properties (class imbalance, embedding dimension, number of samples per class)?

3. **Batch composition robustness**: How does performance degrade with imbalanced batches? Can you quantify the performance penalty when class sizes deviate from M? Can you provide experimental validation of the extended ICC formulation on naturally imbalanced datasets?

4. **Simulation generalization**: The simulation uses 8-dimensional Gaussian mixtures with 4 classes. How sensitive are the conclusions to higher dimensionality (e.g., 256D) or different class structures? Do results hold for non-Gaussian distributions?

5. **Architectural dependency analysis**: For Task 3, why did the one- and two-embedding architectures fail to converge? This suggests fundamental conflicts—can you provide theoretical analysis of when and why such incompatibilities arise?

6. **Dimension correlation analysis**: You acknowledge that per-dimension ICC computation may lead to correlated embedding dimensions. Can you quantify this empirically (compute correlation matrices) and demonstrate whether downstream performance is affected by dimension correlation?

7. **Ablation studies**: Can you provide ablations isolating the contribution of ICC regularization versus improved hyperparameter tuning of the contrastive loss alone? How does ICC performance scale with embedding dimension L, batch size N, and samples per class M?

8. **Statistical significance**: For Tasks 1-2, can you provide confidence intervals and significance tests (e

reviews.summary:
In this paper, the concept of repeatability from measurement theory was introduced for representation learning. Intraclass Correlation (ICC) was proposed as an evaluation metric and the ICC regularizer was used as an additional element to contrastive loss for training. This aims to enhance the repeatability of deep-learned embeddings.
Some synthetic examples and intuitive reasoning were used to illustrate why the ICC regularizer is superior in minimizing intra-class variance compared to contrastive loss. The ICC regularizer was assessed across three speech tasks: speaker embeddings for Text-Independent Speaker Verification (TI-SV) and zero-shot voice style conversion, along with voice feature embeddings for a clinical application. The experiments indicate that the ICC regularizer can boost the repeatability of learned embeddings and perform better in downstream tasks.
reviews.strengths:
The paper proposed a novel metric of ICC based on measurement repeatability. Although the term may be a little confusing, the motivation 
and intuition were well explained. The experimental results on three speaker/speech tasks are convincing.
reviews.weaknesses:
To show the repeatability of speaker embedding for TI-SV task, more controlled experiments may be necessary to prove the effectiveness of ICC loss in addition to the ICC scores: for example, adding noise to the testing sample, or switching languages of the same speaker as it was tested in the NIST Speaker Recognition Evaluation. 

For results on dysphonic voice detection in Table 3, other baseline models were definitely over-trained compared to the proposed model.
Why do you train your own models instead of quoting the numbers from the original papers? (see line 334-335). The results in Table 3 may be unfair comparisons.
reviews.questions: See the comments above in the weakness section.
reviews.limitations: No discussion on negative societal impact was included in the paper
