- Line 24: to cache -> caching
- Diagnostic task is run for a maximum length of 1000
    - This is achievable using untruncated BPTT. Since BPTT scales with sequence length and RTRL does not, it would make more sense to do a very large sequence length.
- It is not clear to me why varying $M$ matters for RTRL if training is not "online" in the sense that the rollout workers are not updating weights
reviews.limitations: - RTRL is relatively understudied and thus has numerous limitations in its current state. As it receives further attention, I suspect such limitations will be mitigated. It is nice to see the authors be honest about the limitations of RTRL throughout the paper.

Summary:
The authors investigate Realtime Recurrent Learning (RTRL) applied to recurrent RL, in contrast with traditional Backpropagation Through Time (BPTT). The authors' key insight is that they can significantly reduce time and space complexity by using single-layer elementwise recurrence. They propose an elementwise-LSTM (eLSTM), similar to IndRNN, for this purpose.

The authors execute a toy diagnostic task, then use IMPALA and R2D2 to train two variants of their eLSTM on pixels tasks -- one variant using BPTT and the other using RTRL.

As expected, the authors find that increasing the TBPTT length to large values provides the same performance as RTRL. Their is a notable performance gap when using short TBPTT, motivating the use of their approach. Although the tasks here do not necessarily require RTRL, one can imagine POMDPs that span hundreds of thousands of timesteps where this could be useful.

Strengths:
- The authors present an interesting and forward-looking idea. Although we are not aware of any common RL tasks today that require such long-term backpropgatation, it's clear that human-level decision making happens over timeframes much too long for BPTT to be tractable and for TBPTT to capture such dependencies. We suspect methods like RTRL will be necessary for truly intelligent agents.
- The paper is generally well written, and it appears that the authors have dilligently reviewed prior literature.

Weaknesses:
- My biggest complaint is that there is no data showing empirical time or space efficiency of RTRL. My understanding is that the whole point of RTRL is decoupling training space efficiency from the sequence length. I do not expect RTRL to be faster for such short sequences, but I would like to compare practical wall-clock time or GPU memory usage.

- I think it should be made explicit that in the traditional RL scenario (rollout workers separate from trainer, sync weights from trainer to workers every update), BPTT and RTRL are equivalent. It is really only the BPTT truncation that causes issues.

- The paper could benefit from a mini-study further examining the effects of TBPTT vs BPTT. The watermaze/chaser experiments do this, but only for a very limited number of truncation lengths. It would be interesting to see results with truncation lengths at 10, 20, ... 200.

Questions:
- Line 24: to cache -> caching
- Diagnostic task is run for a maximum length of 1000
    - This is achievable using untruncated BPTT. Since BPTT scales with sequence length and RTRL does not, it would make more sense to do a very large sequence length.
- It is not clear to me why varying $M$ matters for RTRL if training is not "online" in the sense that the rollout workers are not updating weights

Limitations:
- RTRL is relatively understudied and thus has numerous limitations in its current state. As it receives further attention, I suspect such limitations will be mitigated. It is nice to see the authors be honest about the limitations of RTRL throughout the paper.

Rating:
7: Accept: Technically solid paper, with high impact on at least one sub-area, or moderate-to-high impact on more than one areas, with good-to-excellent evaluation, resources, reproducibility, and no unaddressed ethical considerations.
reviews.text:
# Hybrid Peer Review: Real-Time Recurrent Learning for Recurrent RL

## Summary

This paper investigates the practical promise of Real-Time Recurrent Learning (RTRL) applied to recurrent neural networks in reinforcement learning, moving beyond diagnostic tasks to realistic settings. The authors focus on RNN architectures with element-wise recurrence (eLSTM), which enable tractable RTRL without approximation, reducing complexity from O(N⁴) time and O(N³) space (fully recurrent case) to O(N²) for both dimensions. They propose R2AC, an actor-critic algorithm combining RTRL with policy gradients, and evaluate it on DMLab-30, ProcGen, and Atari environments. On DMLab memory tasks, their system achieves competitive or superior performance to IMPALA and R2D2 baselines using fewer environmental frames. The paper also candidly discusses previously overlooked limitations of RTRL, particularly the intractability of multi-layer cases and practical challenges for scaling to realistic deep architectures.

## Strengths

The paper addresses an important and underexplored question—whether RTRL can provide practical benefits in realistic RL settings beyond toy diagnostic tasks. This is a valuable empirical contribution to an understudied area. The authors present an interesting and forward-looking idea: although current RL tasks may not strictly require such long-term backpropagation, human-level decision making occurs over timeframes far too long for BPTT to be tractable, suggesting that methods like RTRL will likely be necessary for truly intelligent agents.

The focus on element-wise recurrent architectures is pragmatic. While the core observation that element-wise recurrence enables tractable RTRL dates to Mozer (1989) and is acknowledged as non-novel, the paper usefully identifies that modern architectures—including Quasi-RNNs, Simple Recurrent Units, and Linear Transformers—naturally admit tractable RTRL without approximations. This pragmatic focus cleanly isolates the effect of untruncated gradients from approximation quality, which is a meaningful methodological contribution.

The experimental evaluation on challenging RL benchmarks (DMLab-30, ProcGen, Atari) is substantially more convincing and realistic than typical RTRL work limited to diagnostic tasks. The paper is generally well-written with clear mathematical exposition and detailed derivations. The authors have diligently reviewed prior literature and present their work with appropriate context. The experimental comparison is well-controlled—RTRL and TBPTT use identical architectures and hyperparameters, differing only in gradient computation, which enables fair comparison.

Most notably, the DMLab watermaze results demonstrating RTRL outperformance with fewer environmental frames are noteworthy and suggest genuine practical benefits for long-horizon credit assignment. The authors are commendably honest about limitations, devoting substantial discussion to previously unaddressed challenges, particularly the multi-layer case, which is rarely discussed in prior work.

## Major Weaknesses

### 1. Missing Empirical Efficiency Analysis

The most significant weakness is the absence of empirical data demonstrating time and space efficiency of RTRL. The whole conceptual point of RTRL is decoupling training space efficiency from sequence length. While the authors provide clear theoretical complexity analysis (O(N²) per step for RTRL vs. O(N) for TBPTT), they do not provide practical wall-clock time comparisons, GPU memory usage measurements, or actual computational efficiency metrics. This is critical because constant factors, cache efficiency, GPU utilization, and hardware-specific optimizations can substantially affect practical performance. The authors acknowledge that "hardware improvements may favor TBPTT," but this concern is not empirically addressed. Without such measurements, readers cannot assess whether RTRL's theoretical complexity advantages translate to practical computational benefits.

### 2. Severe Architectural Restrictions and Limited Applicability

The restriction to single-layer recurrent networks fundamentally constrains practical applicability. The multi-layer case analysis (Section 5) reveals that stacking layers reintroduces full recurrence complexity, requiring O(L(L+1)/2) sensitivity matrices for L-layer networks versus linear complexity for BPTT. This is a critical limitation that severely restricts deployment in modern deep learning, where stacking layers is standard practice.

More problematically, in realistic RL systems, vision modules must be trained with TBPTT, undermining claims about RTRL's advantages. The paper trains vision stems with truncated gradients while using RTRL only for the recurrent layer, creating a hybrid system where most parameters are trained with TBPTT. This significantly reduces the scope of RTRL's practical applicability and should be more prominently emphasized. The authors do not quantify what fraction of total parameters are trained with each method, nor do they investigate whether joint training of vision modules using RTRL would be feasible or beneficial.

### 3. Mixed and Limited Experimental Results

Benefits appear primarily in watermaze with longer episodes; on other tested environments, results are less compelling. On rooms_select_nonmatching_object, RTRL and TBPTT are nearly identical even with large M values. The ProcGen results are limited to one environment (Chaser) showing benefits; most ProcGen tasks show no meaningful differences between RTRL and TBPTT. Atari results show mixed performance with no consistent advantage—MsPacman actually shows degradation with RTRL, which is not adequately explained. Given that only two of thirty DMLab memory tasks tested show clear RTRL benefits, the generalizability of the approach remains questionable.

Additionally, the paper notes that on ProcGen, feedforward and LSTM baselines perform similarly even in memory-mode, suggesting that recurrence itself may not be the bottleneck on these tasks. This observation weakens the broader case for RTRL's practical necessity.

### 4. Incomplete Experimental Setup and Confounds

Several experimental design issues limit confidence in the results:

- The comparison with R2D2 uses different action repetitions (4 vs. none) and different BPTT spans (M vs. 80), making direct comparison problematic and potentially biasing results in RTRL's favor.
- Pre-training vision modules on TBPTT before evaluation introduces confounds that are difficult to disentangle from the benefits of RTRL.
- Only three random seeds are used, and no statistical significance testing is provided, making it unclear whether observed differences are meaningful.
- The M parameter conflates two variables: update frequency and BPTT span. Decoupling these would better isolate the benefit of untruncated gradients.

### 5. Insufficient Analysis of Sensitivity Matrix Staleness

The paper mentions in Section 3.2 that sensitivity matrices may become stale when weights are updated frequently, but this critical issue is not empirically investigated. As M increases (more frequent updates), the sensitivity matrix computed at earlier timesteps becomes increasingly outdated relative to current weights. The paper provides no quantitative analysis of how this staleness affects learning dynamics, convergence, or final performance. This is a significant gap given that sensitivity matrix staleness could substantially diminish RTRL's practical advantages.

## Minor Weaknesses and Questions

**Limited novelty in core techniques**: While the application to modern architectures is useful, the core element-wise recurrence RTRL derivation is acknowledged as non-novel, dating to Mozer (1989). The contribution is primarily empirical rather than methodological.

**Unclear practical guidance**: The paper does not clearly establish when practitioners should use RTRL over simply increasing BPTT span, especially as hardware continues to improve. Many tested tasks do not absolutely require RTRL; TBPTT with sufficient span suffices, suggesting limited practical necessity in current settings.

**Missing ablations and analysis**: 
- How does sensitivity matrix staleness empirically affect performance as M increases? Learning curves isolating this effect would be valuable.
- Have you investigated hybrid approaches (e.g., RTRL for certain layers, TBPTT for others) more systematically?
- For the eLSTM's representational limitations (acknowledged in Appendix B.1), how does performance compare to multi-layer LSTM with TBPTT on these same tasks?

**Limited scope of memory tasks**: Only two DMLab memory environments are tested. Broader evaluation on additional memory tasks would strengthen claims about when RTRL provides benefits.

**Incomplete comparisons**: No comparison with recent approximation-based RTRL methods is provided. Additionally, the Linear Transformer RTRL (Appendix A.2) is not included in main experiments despite potentially offering advantages.

**Technical clarification needed**: It should be made explicit that in the traditional RL scenario with rollout workers separate from trainers (where weights are synchronized between workers and trainer), BPTT and RTRL are equivalent in practice. It is really only BPTT truncation that causes issues. This context would help readers understand when RTRL's advantages actually matter.

**Diagnostic task design**: The diagnostic task is run for a maximum length of 1000 timesteps, which is achievable using untruncated BPTT. Since BPTT scales

This paper explores an alternative to back-propagation through time (BPTT) for training recurrent neural networks, called real time recurrent learning (RTRL). The alternative algorithm does not depend on past activations, and also does not need truncation (which limits past context) like in BPTT to make it tractable. Although RTRL is very computationally complex for the general case, this paper exploits the fact that several recent recurrent architectures (Quasi-RNNs) allow for exact RTRL gradients to be computed efficiently. The method is evaluated on reinforcement learning tasks on several benchmarks and is compared with truncated BPTT  with LSTM while using Actor-Critic method as RL algorithms.

Strengths:
1.	The paper studies a very interesting direction of using RTRL instead of truncated BPTT. The difficulty of using BPTT for training recurrent architectures is well known and it might be a potential bottleneck in allowing recurrent networks in achieving their full performance in complex tasks in the real-world.  
2.	The paper is written in a very clear way, clearly outlining the core contributions and the focus of this work.
3.	The proposed method is very clean, elegant, well-explained, and simple. 
4.	The empirical evaluation is done very extensively on DMLab, ProcGen and Atari, and it's interesting to see that RTRL + A2C performs equivalent, or better than TBPTT, except for in a few scenarios. 
5.	The limitations section is well-written and shows a good understanding of the proposed technique.

Weaknesses:
Weaknesses / Questions:
1. Ablate the dependence of M (episode rollout length) on R2AC. Since larger M leads to less frequent updates and shorter M leads to stale values in the sensitivity matrix, it would be interesting to see how the performance varies with this parameter. 
2. One modification to the empirical evaluation that I would suggest is to compare with LSTM (instead of eLSTM), and also to compare the best performance for both the training algorithms – TBPTT and R2AC for any value of M, tuned separately for the two algorithms.
3. How does BPTT + LSTM with multiple hidden layers perform in the considered benchmarks?

Questions:
(see weaknesses section for questions)

The limitations section is well-written and gives a clear explanation of the limits of the proposed line of work to train recurrent networks.

reviews.text:
# Hybrid Peer Review

## Summary

This paper investigates the practical promise of Real-Time Recurrent Learning (RTRL) as an alternative to truncated Back-Propagation Through Time (BPTT) for training recurrent neural networks in realistic settings. Rather than relying on past activations and requiring truncation to remain tractable, RTRL computes exact gradients in real-time. The authors focus on element-wise recurrent architectures (eLSTM)—including Quasi-RNNs, Simple Recurrent Units, and Linear Transformers—where RTRL becomes computationally tractable without approximation, reducing complexity from O(N⁴) time and O(N³) space in the fully recurrent case to O(N²) for both. They develop R2AC, an actor-critic algorithm combining RTRL with policy gradients, and evaluate it extensively on DMLab-30, ProcGen, and Atari environments. On DMLab memory tasks, R2AC achieves competitive or superior performance compared to IMPALA and R2D2 baselines while using fewer environmental frames. The paper also candidly discusses previously overlooked limitations of RTRL, particularly the fundamental intractability of multi-layer architectures.

## Strengths

1. **Important research direction**: The paper addresses a significant and underexplored question—the true empirical promise of RTRL beyond toy diagnostic tasks. This is valuable given that the limitations of BPTT for training recurrent architectures are well-known and represent a potential bottleneck in achieving full performance on complex real-world tasks. Most prior RTRL work focuses on approximation methods in synthetic settings, making this empirical evaluation in realistic environments a meaningful contribution.

2. **Pragmatic architectural focus**: While the authors acknowledge that element-wise recurrence enabling tractable RTRL dates back to Mozer (1989), the observation that modern architectures naturally admit exact RTRL without approximations is useful and well-executed. This design choice is pragmatic—it enables clean isolation of the effect of untruncated gradients from confounds introduced by approximation quality, which has been a persistent issue in prior RTRL research.

3. **Clear presentation and mathematical exposition**: The paper is exceptionally well-written with clear mathematical derivations. The core contributions are outlined explicitly, the proposed method is elegant and simple, and the eLSTM formulation is presented with tractable forward recursions that are easy to follow. The complexity analysis is concrete and helpful.

4. **Careful experimental design**: The empirical evaluation is extensive and well-controlled. RTRL and TBPTT use identical architectures and hyperparameters, differing only in gradient computation, which enables fair comparison. The evaluation spans multiple challenging benchmarks (DMLab-30, ProcGen, Atari-2600), and the results demonstrating that R2AC performs equivalent to or better than TBPTT in many scenarios is noteworthy.

5. **Honest treatment of limitations**: The paper is commendably transparent about the constraints of the approach. The limitations section is well-written and demonstrates genuine understanding of the technique. Notably, the discussion of multi-layer RTRL intractability (Section 5) provides valuable insights rarely addressed in prior work. The authors clearly explain why stacking element-wise layers reintroduces full recurrence complexity and quantify the O(L(L+1)/2) sensitivity matrix requirement for L-layer networks.

6. **Concrete empirical benefits**: Results on the DMLab watermaze task demonstrate meaningful practical benefits of untruncated gradients, where RTRL achieves superior performance using substantially fewer environmental frames. This suggests genuine advantages in long-horizon credit assignment scenarios.

## Weaknesses

1. **Limited technical novelty**: The core insight that element-wise recurrence enables tractable RTRL is acknowledged as non-novel, tracing back to Mozer (1989). While the application to modern architectures (Quasi-RNN, SRU, Linear Transformers) is useful, the conceptual and technical contribution remains incremental. The main contribution is empirical evaluation rather than methodological innovation.

2. **Severe architectural constraints limit practical applicability**: The restriction to single-layer recurrent networks is a critical limitation. The multi-layer analysis (Section 5) reveals fundamental intractability—the O(L²) scaling of sensitivity matrices for L-layer networks is prohibitive. This severely constrains applicability in modern deep learning, where stacking layers is standard practice. The authors should more forcefully emphasize that this fundamentally limits practical deployment.

3. **Incomplete experimental scope and mixed results**: While the empirical evaluation is extensive, the benefits of RTRL are limited in scope. On DMLab, only 2 of 30 memory tasks show clear RTRL benefits; select_nonmatching_object shows nearly identical performance between RTRL and TBPTT even with large M. ProcGen results are limited to one environment (Chaser) showing benefits; most other environments show no meaningful differences. Atari results are mixed, with MsPacman actually showing degradation for RTRL. The watermaze task appears to be the primary environment where RTRL substantially outperforms TBPTT, raising questions about generalizability. This pattern suggests that RTRL's practical advantages are narrow and task-dependent.

4. **Hybrid implementation undermines claims**: A critical practical limitation is that vision modules must be trained with TBPTT rather than RTRL, due to the multi-layer constraint. This means the implemented system is hybrid, not pure RTRL, which substantially weakens claims about RTRL's advantages. The paper acknowledges this but does not adequately emphasize its impact. Questions remain: What percentage of total parameters are trained with each method? Does this hybrid approach negate RTRL's memory efficiency advantages? The paper would benefit from quantifying how much of the performance gain is attributable to RTRL versus TBPTT components.

5. **Insufficient analysis of sensitivity matrix staleness**: The paper mentions (Section 3.2) that frequent updates may cause the sensitivity matrix to become stale, as it is computed with weights from earlier timesteps. This is a potentially important issue but is not empirically investigated. The paper provides no learning curves, ablation studies, or quantitative analysis isolating this effect. How does performance degrade as M decreases and updates become more frequent? This gap weakens confidence in the practical benefits claimed.

6. **Missing important experimental details and comparisons**: 
   - No wall-clock time or memory consumption comparisons are provided despite theoretical complexity claims. The O(N²) complexity analysis does not account for constant factors, cache efficiency, or GPU utilization, which may favor TBPTT in practice.
   - The comparison with R2D2 uses different action repetitions (4 vs. none) and different BPTT spans (M vs. 80), making direct comparison problematic and potentially biasing results.
   - No comparison with recent approximation-based RTRL methods on the same tasks.
   - Limited investigation of why RTRL helps when it does: the paper lacks deep analysis of which credit assignment paths benefit from untruncated gradients.

7. **Unclear practical necessity and limited guidance**: The paper notes that many tested tasks do not strictly require RTRL—TBPTT with sufficient span suffices, especially as hardware improves. This observation undermines the practical motivation. The paper does not provide clear guidance on when practitioners should choose RTRL over simply increasing BPTT span or using other approaches. Modern deep RL has largely moved away from RNNs toward Transformers or feedforward+memory architectures; the paper does not adequately engage with why RTRL may be solving a diminishing problem.

8. **Potential confounds in experimental setup**: Vision module pre-training and freezing in DMLab experiments may introduce confounds. The paper does not explore whether joint training of vision modules with TBPTT while training recurrent layers with RTRL is necessary, or whether fully RTRL-trained models (even if computationally expensive) would show different results. Additionally, the M parameter conflates update frequency with BPTT span, making it difficult to isolate the benefit of untruncated gradients from effects of update frequency.

## Questions and Suggestions

1. **Ablation on M parameter**: Ablate the dependence of performance on M (episode rollout length). Since larger M leads to less frequent updates and shorter M leads to stale values in the sensitivity matrix, systematic ablation would clarify the staleness effect and help establish optimal settings.

2. **Sensitivity matrix staleness analysis**: Provide empirical quantification of how sensitivity matrix staleness affects performance as update frequency varies. This could include learning curves or performance metrics isolating this effect across different values of M.

3. **Wall-clock time comparisons**: Compare actual computational wall-clock time and memory usage between RTRL and TBPTT implementations, not just theoretical complexity. This would clarify practical efficiency and inform practitioners about real-world performance.

4. **Fairer baseline comparisons**: Compare with standard LSTM (not just eLSTM) and tune both TBPTT and R2AC

Weaknesses / Questions:
1. Ablate the dependence of M (episode rollout length) on R2AC. Since larger M leads to less frequent updates and shorter M leads to stale values in the sensitivity matrix, it would be interesting to see how the performance varies with this parameter. 
2. One modification to the empirical evaluation that I would suggest is to compare with LSTM (instead of eLSTM), and also to compare the best performance for both the training algorithms – TBPTT and R2AC for any value of M, tuned separately for the two algorithms.
3. How does BPTT + LSTM with multiple hidden layers perform in the considered benchmarks?

Questions:
(see weaknesses section for questions)

Limitations:
The limitations section is well-written and gives a clear explanation of the limits of the proposed line of work to train recurrent networks.

Rating:
7: Accept: Technically solid paper, with high impact on at least one sub-area, or moderate-to-high impact on more than one areas, with good-to-excellent evaluation, resources, reproducibility, and no unaddressed ethical considerations.
reviews.original_rating: 7: Accept: Technically solid paper, with high impact on at least one sub-area, or moderate-to-high impact on more than one areas, with good-to-excellent evaluation, resources, reproducibility, and no unaddressed ethical considerations.

reviews.text:
# Hybrid Peer Review

## Summary

This paper investigates the practical promise of Real-Time Recurrent Learning (RTRL) for training recurrent neural networks in realistic settings, moving beyond diagnostic tasks. The authors focus on RNN architectures with element-wise recurrence (eLSTM), which enable tractable RTRL without approximation, achieving O(N²) space and time complexity compared to the prohibitive O(N³) and O(N⁴) of fully recurrent networks. They develop R2AC, an actor-critic algorithm combining RTRL with policy gradients, and evaluate it on DMLab-30, ProcGen, and Atari environments. On DMLab memory tasks, R2AC achieves competitive or superior performance compared to IMPALA and R2D2 baselines while using fewer environmental frames. The paper also discusses previously underexplored limitations of RTRL, particularly the intractability of multi-layer cases.

## Strengths

1. **Addresses an important gap**: The paper tackles a fundamental question—what is the true empirical promise of RTRL in realistic settings?—that has been largely unexplored. Most prior RTRL work focuses on approximation methods in diagnostic tasks, making this evaluation on challenging RL benchmarks substantially more convincing and valuable.

2. **Pragmatic architectural focus**: While the core observation that element-wise recurrence enables tractable RTRL dates to Mozer (1989), the paper usefully identifies that modern architectures (Quasi-RNN, SRU, Linear Transformers) naturally admit exact RTRL computation without approximations. This eliminates confounds from approximation quality and cleanly isolates the effect of untruncated gradients.

3. **Clear mathematical exposition**: The mathematical derivations are clear and detailed, with tractable RTRL formulations provided explicitly. The complexity analysis is well-presented with concrete comparisons between fully recurrent and element-wise cases.

4. **Well-controlled experimental comparison**: RTRL and TBPTT use identical architectures and hyperparameters, differing only in gradient computation. This enables fair comparison and strengthens claims about the benefits of untruncated gradients specifically.

5. **Commendable honesty about limitations**: The paper devotes substantial discussion to previously unaddressed challenges, particularly the multi-layer case (Section 5), which reveals fundamental intractability rarely discussed in prior work. This candid treatment of limitations is valuable for the community.

6. **Meaningful empirical results on memory tasks**: Results on DMLab watermaze demonstrate practical benefits of RTRL for long-horizon credit assignment, with the system achieving competitive performance using fewer environmental frames.

## Major Weaknesses

1. **Limited novelty in core technique**: The authors appropriately acknowledge that the element-wise recurrence RTRL derivation traces back to Mozer (1989), and the technical contribution is largely incremental. While the application to modern architectures is useful, the conceptual novelty is limited. The paper's primary contribution is empirical evaluation rather than methodological innovation.

2. **Severe architectural restrictions fundamentally limit applicability**: The restriction to single-layer networks is a critical limitation for modern deep learning. The multi-layer analysis in Section 5 reveals that stacking layers reintroduces full recurrence complexity, requiring O(L(L+1)/2) sensitivity matrices for L-layer networks. This is a fundamental barrier to scaling that makes the approach impractical for realistic deep architectures where stacking is ubiquitous.

3. **Incomplete experimental coverage with mixed results**: 
   - Only 2 of 30 DMLab tasks (watermaze and select_nonmatching_object) were evaluated, with clear benefits visible primarily in watermaze. Most other environments show no meaningful differences between RTRL and TBPTT.
   - ProcGen results are limited to one environment (Chaser) showing benefits; other games show minimal or no advantage.
   - Atari results show no consistent advantage, with MsPacman actually showing performance degradation.
   - The narrow practical scenario where RTRL provides unambiguous benefit—long-memory tasks where BPTT truncation is the bottleneck—raises questions about generalizability.

4. **Hybrid experimental design undermines RTRL advantage claims**: Vision modules are trained with TBPTT rather than RTRL, significantly limiting the scope of RTRL's applicability. In realistic RL, deep visual feature extraction is essential, and training only the recurrent layer with RTRL while using TBPTT for vision modules may negate most practical benefits. This is a significant compromise that deserves more prominent discussion and quantification.

5. **Experimental setup concerns**:
   - The comparison with R2D2 uses different action repetitions (4 vs. none) and different BPTT spans (M vs. 80), making direct comparison problematic. A more controlled comparison with identical hyperparameters would be more convincing.
   - Pre-training vision modules on TBPTT introduces confounds in the DMLab experiments.
   - Only 3 seeds are used, and no statistical significance testing is provided.
   - The M parameter conflates update frequency and BPTT span, making it difficult to isolate the benefit of untruncated gradients from other factors.

6. **Insufficient analysis of sensitivity matrix staleness**: The paper mentions in Section 3.2 that frequent weight updates may stale the sensitivity matrix, but provides no empirical investigation of this potential issue. This is a critical practical concern that deserves quantitative analysis, especially as M increases.

## Minor Weaknesses and Missing Elements

1. **Limited scope of evaluation domains**: While experiments on RL tasks are valuable, the paper would benefit from evaluation on other domains such as language modeling or sequential image classification to establish broader applicability. The generalization of RTRL benefits beyond RL is unclear.

2. **Missing important baselines and comparisons**:
   - No BPTT baseline where backpropagation occurs through the entire episode (only truncated BPTT is compared).
   - No comparison with recent approximation-based RTRL methods on the same tasks to contextualize the benefits of exact RTRL.
   - No wall-clock time comparisons despite theoretical complexity claims; only environmental frames are reported. Practical efficiency matters for practitioners, and the paper acknowledges that hardware improvements may favor TBPTT.

3. **Insufficient ablation studies**:
   - No systematic study of sensitivity to the M parameter (update frequency/BPTT span).
   - No empirical investigation of sensitivity matrix staleness effects.
   - Limited analysis of why RTRL helps when it does; the paper doesn't deeply investigate which credit assignment paths benefit from untruncated gradients.

4. **Representational capacity not thoroughly addressed**: The restriction to element-wise recurrence may limit model expressiveness on certain tasks. While Appendix B.1 provides a synthetic example, broader quantification of the expressiveness-performance tradeoff relative to full LSTM would strengthen claims about practical viability.

5. **Unclear practical guidance**: The paper notes that many tested tasks don't strictly require RTRL—TBPTT with sufficient span suffices, especially as hardware improves—but doesn't clearly establish when practitioners should choose RTRL over simply increasing BPTT span. This guidance is vague and limits practical impact.

6. **Minor factual issue**: The statement that "Silver et al. [12] explore random projections of the sensitivity" is incomplete. The DODGE work explores not only random projections but also learning candidate directions through auxiliary tasks or synthetic gradients.

## Questions for Authors

1. How does sensitivity matrix staleness empirically affect performance as M increases? Can you provide learning curves or final performance metrics isolating this effect?

2. What percentage of total parameters are trained with RTRL versus TBPTT in your experiments? Does the hybrid approach of training vision stems with TBPTT negate RTRL's memory efficiency advantages?

3. Can you provide wall-clock time and memory consumption comparisons between RTRL and TBPTT? The theoretical complexity analysis is useful, but practical efficiency matters for practitioners.

4. For the DMLab experiments, how sensitive are results to freezing the vision module versus training it with TBPTT? Have you experimented with fully RTRL-trained models, even if computationally expensive?

5. Can you provide more controlled comparisons with R2D2 using identical hyperparameters (action repetition, BPTT span)?

6. Why does RTRL show benefits only for watermaze but not select_nonmatching_object? Is this primarily due to episode length differences, or are there other task characteristics that determine when RTRL helps?

7. Have you investigated hybrid approaches (e.g., RTRL for recurrent layers, TBPTT for feedforward layers) more systematically?

8. Can you evaluate the method on additional domains (language modeling, sequential image classification) and provide comparison to SnAp-1 or other recent methods?

## Limitations

The authors adequately identify several critical limitations, though some deserve expanded discussion:

Summary:
- The paper explores the use of RTRL in the case of scenario, where no approximation is needed.
- The proposed "method" is not novel per se i.e., Mozer et. al put forward the derivation of RTRL for element-wise recurrence, but to the best of reviewer's ability no one has formally wrote about the tractability of the RTRL in case of some of the RNN architectures (Quasi-RNN or Simple Recurrent Units).
- The paper does experiments for RL, where on different experiments the paper shows the method trained with RTRL can match or even sometimes outperform Truncated Backprop through time. 

==== 

Read the author's rebuttal.

Strengths:
- The paper is very well written.
- The idea of using RTRL for RNNs in which element-wise recurrent is present is interesting.
- The paper does experiments on many RL tasks, and compares the proposed method to TBPTT.

Weaknesses:
- Since the paper is about how well RTRL works with some RNNs, it will be useful to evaluate the proposed method on more tasks like language modelling or sequential image classification etc. 
- For all the experiments, it will also be useful to report a BPTT baseline i.e., where we backprop through the entire "episode". 
- It will also be useful to compare to various other approximations of RTRL (using the vanilla LSTM/GRU) or even consider the tasks consider in SnAp-1. 
- Not a weakness per se, but this is incorrect "Silver et al. [12] explore random projections of the sensitivity". DODGE work explores not just the random projections, but also learning the candidate direction (as a result of auxiliary tasks or via RL like synthetic gradients).

Questions:
- Evaluate the proposed method on more tasks like language modelling, sequential image classification as well as compare to SnAp-1.
- Report the BPTT baseline, as well as some approximation of RTRL to just see how well those approximations work for the problems chosen in this work. 
- It will be helpful to make sure similar setup is followed throughout the experiments. For example: for some experiments, the paper uses the pre-trained visual encoder, or trains the visual encoder via the truncated BPTT gradient.

Limitations:
See Weaknesses and Questions.

Rating:
4: Borderline reject: Technically solid paper where reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation, outweigh reasons to accept, e.g., good evaluation. Please use sparingly.

reviews.summary: The paper modifies the LSTM architecture so that RTRL complexity, which is usually O(n^4), becomes O(n^2) where n is the number of recurrent units. This is achieved by constraining the recurrent weights to a diagonal structure. After performing some diagnostic tests, the paper presents experimental results using the proposed eLSTM in actor-critic RL settings, specifically DMLab, ProcGen, and Atari. The paper concludes with a discussion on the complexity of RTRL in the multi-layer case.
reviews.strengths:
The paper is well written and clearly structured. 

While the ideas presented in the paper are not novel per se, the application of these ideas to a modern RNN architecture in the context of RL is quite novel. 

The work is quite relevant for the RNN/RL communities as unlocking RTRL for large-scale recurrent models is a long-standing problem and RL is an important field of application for RNNs. 

The paper discusses some rarely addressed limitations about the complexity of RTRL in the multi-layer case as well as some other interesting points.
reviews.weaknesses:
The main weakness of the paper is the empirical evaluation of the method. This ranges from an incrompehensible selection of tasks over a severe lack of external baselines to insufficient evaluation methods (e.g., lack of significance testing). In the following, I will elaborate in greater detail on these and other shortcomings. 

On Procgen and Atari, the paper lacks comparison to state-of-the-art methods or any other external baselines. In general, the authors seem to confuse the concepts of ablation studies and external baselines. For instance, TBPTT on the eLSTM architecture is an ablation study while TBPTT on the fully recurrent LSTM architecture is a baseline (which is missing in all experiments but should be included; see, e.g., [3] for design choices). The authors should compare to state-of-the-art baselines in all environments, not just DMLab-30. 

The paper should experimentally explore the limitations arising from the diagonal constraint on the hidden-to-hidden interactions. This limitation competes with the bias in the gradient estimate used by the companion learning algorithm and SnAp-1 for LSTM. The authors should compare these RTRL variants in experiments to justify their proposed method as opposed to existing ones. 

The core mechanism described in Eq. (5) that reduces the RTRL complexity has been proposed in [1] and should be cited properly. 

In all RL experiments, the authors report mean and standard deviation over only 3 seeds (at least for DMLab-30 and Procgen; for Atari I didn't find any info) without any significance testing. In particular, the use of the standard deviation as a measure of uncertainty in combination with a low number of seeds has been critisized as bad practice and should be replaced by interval estimates such as IQM [2]. 

The paper states that it focuses on "RTRL-based algorithms beyond diagnostic tasks." However, it limits these experiments to the realm of RL, where the importance of memory is often unclear. The authors should investigate how the architectural changes affect performance in some standard supervised tasks. 

[1] Felix A. Gers, Jürgen Schmidhuber:
Recurrent Nets that Time and Count. IJCNN (3) 2000: 189-194

[2] Rishabh Agarwal, Max Schwarzer, Pablo Samuel Castro, Aaron C. Courville, Marc G. Bellemare:
Deep Reinforcement Learning at the Edge of the Statistical Precipice. NeurIPS 2021: 29304-29320

[3] Tianwei Ni, Benjamin Eysenbach, Ruslan Salakhutdinov:
Recurrent Model-Free RL Can Be a Strong Baseline for Many POMDPs. ICML 2022: 16691-16723
reviews.questions:
The proposed eLSTM architecture does not use any cell state activation function. This does not affect the complexity of RTRL so I wonder why this slightly off-standard decision was made. 

Line 21, 53, and 290: shouldn't the word "quadratic" be replaced by "quartic"? 

Why did the authors dedicate resources to reactive tasks where no benefit are to be expected or an RNN might not be helpful to begin with? I had the impression that resources for experiments are limited. This exacerbates the question why reactive tasks were explored. 

The paper states its focus is "to evaluate learning with untruncated gradients rather than the potential for online learning." I wonder on what basis this decision was made as online updates could lead to high sample efficiency, one of the key problems in RL. I feel the paper missed a big chance by not researching this connection of online updates and sample efficiency. Instead, it focussed on reactive tasks. 

The paper states that "both the feedforward and LSTM baselines perform similarly" in Procgen Memory mode. However, [5] shows that the feedforward baseline is outperformed by methods using memory in 4/6 environments. Moreover, the Procgen paper [4] (see appendix H) shows that LSTM or framestacking outperforms feedforward in 4/6 envirnments even in non-memory mode. 

In summary, I really like the presented method (and the way it was presented) and believe that it bears quite some potential. However, the empirical evaluation is very weak and I do not believe it can be fixed in the short rebuttal period. Therefore, publication of the paper seems premature at this point. 

[4] Karl Cobbe, Christopher Hesse, Jacob Hilton, John Schulman:
Leveraging Procedural Generation to Benchmark Reinforcement Learning. ICML 2020: 2048-2056

[5] Fabian Paischer, Thomas Adler, Vihang P. Patil, Angela Bitto-Nemling, Markus Holzleitner, Sebastian Lehner, Hamid Eghbal-Zadeh, Sepp Hochreiter:
History Compression via Language Models in Reinforcement Learning. ICML 2022: 17156-17185
reviews.limitations: The authors give some interesting discussion on the limitation of their work, in particular the multi-layer RNN case.
reviews.rating: 4: Borderline reject: Technically solid paper where reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation, outweigh reasons to accept, e.g., good evaluation. Please use sparingly.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
The paper modifies the LSTM architecture so that RTRL complexity, which is usually O(n^4), becomes O(n^2) where n is the number of recurrent units. This is achieved by constraining the recurrent weights to a diagonal structure. After performing some diagnostic tests, the paper presents experimental results using the proposed eLSTM in actor-critic RL settings, specifically DMLab, ProcGen, and Atari. The paper concludes with a discussion on the complexity of RTRL in the multi-layer case.

Strengths:
The paper is well written and clearly structured. 

While the ideas presented in the paper are not novel per se, the application of these ideas to a modern RNN architecture in the context of RL is quite novel. 

The work is quite relevant for the RNN/RL communities as unlocking RTRL for large-scale recurrent models is a long-standing problem and RL is an important field of application for RNNs. 

The paper discusses some rarely addressed limitations about the complexity of RTRL in the multi-layer case as well as some other interesting points.

Weaknesses:
The main weakness of the paper is the empirical evaluation of the method. This ranges from an incrompehensible selection of tasks over a severe lack of external baselines to insufficient evaluation methods (e.g., lack of significance testing). In the following, I will elaborate in greater detail on these and other shortcomings. 

On Procgen and Atari, the paper lacks comparison to state-of-the-art methods or any other external baselines. In general, the authors seem to confuse the concepts of ablation studies and external baselines. For instance, TBPTT on the eLSTM architecture is an ablation study while TBPTT on the fully recurrent LSTM architecture is a baseline (which is missing in all experiments but should be included; see, e.g., [3] for design choices). The authors should compare to state-of-the-art baselines in all environments, not just DMLab-30. 

The paper should experimentally explore the limitations arising from the diagonal constraint on the hidden-to-hidden interactions. This limitation competes with the bias in the gradient estimate used by the companion learning algorithm and SnAp-1 for LSTM. The authors should compare these RTRL variants in experiments to justify their proposed method as opposed to existing ones. 

The core mechanism described in Eq. (5) that reduces the RTRL complexity has been proposed in [1] and should be cited properly. 

In all RL experiments, the authors report mean and standard deviation over only 3 seeds (at least for DMLab-30 and Procgen; for Atari I didn't find any info) without any significance testing. In particular, the use of the standard deviation as a measure of uncertainty in combination with a low number of seeds has been critisized as bad practice and should be replaced by interval estimates such as IQM [2]. 

The paper states that it focuses on "RTRL-based algorithms beyond diagnostic tasks." However, it limits these experiments to the realm of RL, where the importance of memory is often unclear. The authors should investigate how the architectural changes affect performance in some standard supervised tasks. 

[1] Felix A. Gers, Jürgen Schmidhuber:
Recurrent Nets that Time and Count. IJCNN (3) 2000: 189-194

[2] Rishabh Agarwal, Max Schwarzer, Pablo Samuel Castro, Aaron C. Courville, Marc G. Bellemare:
Deep Reinforcement Learning at the Edge of the Statistical Precipice. NeurIPS 2021: 29304-29320

[3] Tianwei Ni, Benjamin Eysenbach, Ruslan Salakhutdinov:
Recurrent Model-Free RL Can Be a Strong Baseline for Many POMDPs. ICML 2022: 16691-16723

Questions:
The proposed eLSTM architecture does not use any cell state activation function. This does not affect the complexity of RTRL so I wonder why this slightly off-standard decision was made. 

Line 21, 53, and 290: shouldn't the word "quadratic" be replaced by "quartic"? 

Why did the authors dedicate resources to reactive tasks where no benefit are to be expected or an RNN might not be helpful to begin with? I had the impression that resources for experiments are limited. This exacerbates the question why reactive tasks were explored. 

The paper states its focus is "to evaluate learning with untruncated gradients rather than the potential for online learning." I wonder on what basis this decision was made as online updates could lead to high sample efficiency, one of the key problems in RL. I feel the paper missed a big chance by not researching this connection of online updates and sample efficiency. Instead, it focussed on reactive tasks. 

The paper states that "both the feedforward and LSTM baselines perform similarly" in Procgen Memory mode. However, [5] shows that the feedforward baseline is outperformed by methods using memory in 4/6 environments. Moreover, the Procgen paper [4] (see appendix H) shows that LSTM or framestacking outperforms feedforward in 4/6 envirnments even in non-memory mode. 

In summary, I really like the presented method (and the way it was presented) and believe that it bears quite some potential. However, the empirical evaluation is very weak and I do not believe it can be fixed in the short rebuttal period. Therefore, publication of the paper seems premature at this point. 

[4] Karl Cobbe, Christopher Hesse, Jacob Hilton, John Schulman:
Leveraging Procedural Generation to Benchmark Reinforcement Learning. ICML 2020: 2048-2056

[5] Fabian Paischer, Thomas Adler, Vihang P. Patil, Angela Bitto-Nemling, Markus Holzleitner, Sebastian Lehner, Hamid Eghbal-Zadeh, Sepp Hochreiter:
History Compression via Language Models in Reinforcement Learning. ICML 2022: 17156-17185

Limitations:
The authors give some interesting discussion on the limitation of their work, in particular the multi-layer RNN case.

Rating:
4: Borderline reject: Technically solid paper where reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation, outweigh reasons to accept, e.g., good evaluation. Please use sparingly.
reviews.text:
# Hybrid Peer Review

## Summary

This paper addresses the long-standing problem of making Real-Time Recurrent Learning (RTRL) tractable for practical applications by proposing eLSTM, a modified LSTM architecture with diagonal recurrent weight constraints. This architectural modification reduces RTRL complexity from O(n^4) to O(n^2), where n is the number of recurrent units. The authors develop R2AC, an actor-critic algorithm combining RTRL with policy gradients, and evaluate it on RL benchmarks including DMLab-30, ProcGen, and Atari environments. The paper is well-written and clearly structured, with commendably honest discussion of previously underexplored limitations, particularly regarding multi-layer architectures.

## Strengths

The paper makes several valuable contributions. First, it addresses an important gap by evaluating RTRL beyond toy diagnostic tasks in realistic RL environments, providing empirical insights that are substantially more convincing than prior work limited to synthetic problems. Second, while the element-wise recurrence mechanism is not conceptually novel (tracing back to Mozer 1989), the pragmatic application to modern RNN architectures (Quasi-RNN, SRU, Linear Transformers) in the context of RL is genuinely useful and timely. The focus on element-wise recurrent architectures is particularly valuable because it enables exact gradient computation without approximations, cleanly isolating the effect of untruncated gradients from approximation quality—a confound that has plagued prior RTRL work.

Third, the experimental evaluation on challenging benchmarks is substantially more realistic than typical RTRL work. The DMLab watermaze results demonstrating RTRL outperformance with fewer environmental frames are noteworthy and suggest practical benefits for long-horizon credit assignment. Fourth, the mathematical exposition is clear and detailed, with tractable RTRL formulations provided explicitly and concrete complexity analysis. The experimental comparison is well-controlled, with RTRL and TBPTT using identical architectures and hyperparameters, differing only in gradient truncation.

Finally, the paper deserves credit for its candid discussion of limitations. The treatment of multi-layer RTRL intractability (Section 5) provides valuable insights rarely addressed in prior work, and the authors' honesty about the restricted applicability of their method is commendable.

## Major Weaknesses

Despite these strengths, the empirical evaluation is substantially limited and represents the paper's most critical shortcoming. This limitation manifests across multiple dimensions:

**Incomplete Baseline Comparisons and Experimental Design:** The paper lacks comparison to state-of-the-art methods and external baselines in most experiments. Critically, the authors appear to conflate ablation studies with external baselines. For instance, TBPTT on the eLSTM architecture is an ablation study, while TBPTT on a fully recurrent LSTM would be a proper baseline—yet the latter is missing from all experiments despite being essential for understanding the performance trade-offs between architectural constraints and gradient truncation. On ProcGen and Atari, no comparison to state-of-the-art methods is provided. The comparison with R2D2 on DMLab uses different action repetitions and BPTT spans, making direct comparison problematic and undermining the validity of the conclusions.

Moreover, the paper states its focus is "to evaluate learning with untruncated gradients rather than the potential for online learning," yet this decision lacks clear justification given that online updates could lead to high sample efficiency—one of the key problems in RL. This represents a missed opportunity to investigate a potentially important practical advantage of RTRL.

**Architectural Constraints and Practical Limitations:** The restriction to single-layer networks fundamentally constrains applicability. Modern deep learning heavily relies on stacking layers, and the multi-layer analysis reveals that sensitivity matrices scale as O(L(L+1)/2) for L-layer networks, making the approach impractical for realistic deep architectures. More critically, in all realistic RL experiments, vision modules must be trained with TBPTT while only the recurrent layer uses RTRL. This hybrid approach substantially undermines claims about RTRL's advantages, since most parameters in vision-based RL agents are in the feature extraction layers. The paper does not adequately emphasize or quantify this significant practical compromise.

**Mixed and Limited Empirical Results:** The empirical benefits are modest and narrow in scope. On DMLab, only 2 of 30 tasks show clear RTRL benefits; on select_nonmatching_object with large M values, RTRL and TBPTT are nearly identical. ProcGen results are limited to one environment (Chaser) showing benefits, while most other environments show no meaningful differences between RTRL and TBPTT. Atari results show mixed performance with no consistent advantage—notably, MsPacman shows degradation with RTRL. This raises questions about generalizability and suggests that the practical scenario where RTRL provides unambiguous benefit is quite narrow, limited primarily to long-memory tasks where BPTT truncation becomes a bottleneck.

The paper's claim that "both the feedforward and LSTM baselines perform similarly" in Procgen Memory mode warrants scrutiny. Prior work [5] demonstrates that feedforward baselines are outperformed by memory-using methods in 4/6 environments, and the original Procgen paper shows that LSTM or framestacking outperforms feedforward in 4/6 environments even in non-memory mode. This suggests that the paper may have underestimated the importance of memory in these tasks.

**Insufficient Statistical Rigor:** All RL experiments report mean and standard deviation over only 3 seeds (at least for DMLab-30 and Procgen; information for Atari is absent) without any significance testing. This practice has been criticized as problematic and should be replaced with interval estimates such as IQM [2]. With only 3 seeds and no significance testing, it is difficult to assess whether observed differences are meaningful or within noise margins.

**Missing Critical Comparisons and Ablations:** The paper should experimentally explore the limitations arising from the diagonal constraint on hidden-to-hidden interactions. This limitation competes with the bias in gradient estimates used by companion learning algorithms and other RTRL variants (e.g., SnAp-1 for LSTM). The authors should compare these RTRL variants in experiments to justify their proposed method as opposed to existing alternatives. Additionally, the paper mentions sensitivity matrix staleness (from frequent weight updates with old matrix values) in Section 3.2 as a potential issue but provides no empirical investigation of its impact, leaving a significant gap in understanding practical performance.

Furthermore, the paper lacks wall-clock time and memory usage comparisons. While theoretical complexity analysis suggests O(n^2) per step for RTRL versus O(n) for TBPTT (plus caching), constant factors and practical efficiency on actual hardware remain unexplored. This is particularly important given that hardware improvements may favor TBPTT, potentially undermining RTRL's practical relevance.

## Minor Weaknesses and Technical Issues

**Limited Task Diversity and Scope:** The paper states its focus is "RTRL-based algorithms beyond diagnostic tasks," yet limits experiments to RL environments where the importance of memory is often unclear. The authors should investigate how architectural changes affect performance on standard supervised learning tasks (language modeling, machine translation, etc.) where memory is demonstrably critical and BPTT is standard. Additionally, the paper dedicates resources to reactive tasks where no benefit is to be expected or where RNNs might not be helpful. Given apparent resource constraints, this choice seems questionable and exacerbates the concern about limited task diversity.

**Citation and Attribution Issues:** The core mechanism described in Equation (5) that reduces RTRL complexity has been proposed in Mozer (1989) and should be cited more prominently as foundational prior work.

**Presentation Issues:** Lines 21, 53, and 290 use "quadratic" where "quartic" appears more appropriate given the context of complexity reduction.

**Missing Ablations and Analyses:** The paper lacks systematic study of sensitivity to the M parameter (update frequency) and does not provide ablations showing how sensitivity matrix staleness affects performance as M varies. The eLSTM architecture removes the cell state activation function without clear justification—while this doesn't affect RTRL complexity, the design choice deserves explanation.

## Questions for Authors

1. Can you provide a proper baseline using TBPTT on fully recurrent LSTM with identical hyperparameters to isolate the benefit of the architectural constraint from the benefit of untruncated gradients?

2. How much does sensitivity matrix staleness empirically impact learning as update frequency increases? Can you provide quantitative analysis isolating this effect?

3. What percentage of total parameters are trained with RTRL versus TBPTT in your vision-based experiments? Does the hybrid approach negate RTRL's memory efficiency advantages?

4. Can you provide wall-clock time and actual memory consumption comparisons between RTRL and TBPTT implementations on your hardware?

5. Why does RTRL show benefits only for watermaze but not select_nonmatching
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
The paper modifies the LSTM architecture so that RTRL complexity, which is usually O(n^4), becomes O(n^2) where n is the number of recurrent units. This is achieved by constraining the recurrent weights to a diagonal structure. After performing some diagnostic tests, the paper presents experimental results using the proposed eLSTM in actor-critic RL settings, specifically DMLab, ProcGen, and Atari. The paper concludes with a discussion on the complexity of RTRL in the multi-layer case.

Strengths:
The paper is well written and clearly structured. 

While the ideas presented in the paper are not novel per se, the application of these ideas to a modern RNN architecture in the context of RL is quite novel. 

The work is quite relevant for the RNN/RL communities as unlocking RTRL for large-scale recurrent models is a long-standing problem and RL is an important field of application for RNNs. 

The paper discusses some rarely addressed limitations about the complexity of RTRL in the multi-layer case as well as some other interesting points.

Weaknesses:
The main weakness of the paper is the empirical evaluation of the method. This ranges from an incrompehensible selection of tasks over a severe lack of external baselines to insufficient evaluation methods (e.g., lack of significance testing). In the following, I will elaborate in greater detail on these and other shortcomings. 

On Procgen and Atari, the paper lacks comparison to state-of-the-art methods or any other external baselines. In general, the authors seem to confuse the concepts of ablation studies and external baselines. For instance, TBPTT on the eLSTM architecture is an ablation study while TBPTT on the fully recurrent LSTM architecture is a baseline (which is missing in all experiments but should be included; see, e.g., [3] for design choices). The authors should compare to state-of-the-art baselines in all environments, not just DMLab-30. 

The paper should experimentally explore the limitations arising from the diagonal constraint on the hidden-to-hidden interactions. This limitation competes with the bias in the gradient estimate used by the companion learning algorithm and SnAp-1 for LSTM. The authors should compare these RTRL variants in experiments to justify their proposed method as opposed to existing ones. 

The core mechanism described in Eq. (5) that reduces the RTRL complexity has been proposed in [1] and should be cited properly. 

In all RL experiments, the authors report mean and standard deviation over only 3 seeds (at least for DMLab-30 and Procgen; for Atari I didn't find any info) without any significance testing. In particular, the use of the standard deviation as a measure of uncertainty in combination with a low number of seeds has been critisized as bad practice and should be replaced by interval estimates such as IQM [2]. 

The paper states that it focuses on "RTRL-based algorithms beyond diagnostic tasks." However, it limits these experiments to the realm of RL, where the importance of memory is often unclear. The authors should investigate how the architectural changes affect performance in some standard supervised tasks. 

[1] Felix A. Gers, Jürgen Schmidhuber:
Recurrent Nets that Time and Count. IJCNN (3) 2000: 189-194

[2] Rishabh Agarwal, Max Schwarzer, Pablo Samuel Castro, Aaron C. Courville, Marc G. Bellemare:
Deep Reinforcement Learning at the Edge of the Statistical Precipice. NeurIPS 2021: 29304-29320

[3] Tianwei Ni, Benjamin Eysenbach, Ruslan Salakhutdinov:
Recurrent Model-Free RL Can Be a Strong Baseline for Many POMDPs. ICML 2022: 16691-16723

Questions:
The proposed eLSTM architecture does not use any cell state activation function. This does not affect the complexity of RTRL so I wonder why this slightly off-standard decision was made. 

Line 21, 53, and 290: shouldn't the word "quadratic" be replaced by "quartic"? 

Why did the authors dedicate resources to reactive tasks where no benefit are to be expected or an RNN might not be helpful to begin with? I had the impression that resources for experiments are limited. This exacerbates the question why reactive tasks were explored. 

The paper states its focus is "to evaluate learning with untruncated gradients rather than the potential for online learning." I wonder on what basis this decision was made as online updates could lead to high sample efficiency, one of the key problems in RL. I feel the paper missed a big chance by not researching this connection of online updates and sample efficiency. Instead, it focussed on reactive tasks. 

The paper states that "both the feedforward and LSTM baselines perform similarly" in Procgen Memory mode. However, [5] shows that the feedforward baseline is outperformed by methods using memory in 4/6 environments. Moreover, the Procgen paper [4] (see appendix H) shows that LSTM or framestacking outperforms feedforward in 4/6 envirnments even in non-memory mode. 

In summary, I really like the presented method (and the way it was presented) and believe that it bears quite some potential. However, the empirical evaluation is very weak and I do not believe it can be fixed in the short rebuttal period. Therefore, publication of the paper seems premature at this point. 

[4] Karl Cobbe, Christopher Hesse, Jacob Hilton, John Schulman:
Leveraging Procedural Generation to Benchmark Reinforcement Learning. ICML 2020: 2048-2056

[5] Fabian Paischer, Thomas Adler, Vihang P. Patil, Angela Bitto-Nemling, Markus Holzleitner, Sebastian Lehner, Hamid Eghbal-Zadeh, Sepp Hochreiter:
History Compression via Language Models in Reinforcement Learning. ICML 2022: 17156-17185

Limitations:
The authors give some interesting discussion on the limitation of their work, in particular the multi-layer RNN case.

Rating:
4: Borderline reject: Technically solid paper where reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation, outweigh reasons to accept, e.g., good evaluation. Please use sparingly.
reviews.original_rating: 4: Borderline reject: Technically solid paper where reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation, outweigh reasons to accept, e.g., good evaluation. Please use sparingly.

title: Towards a Better Theoretical Understanding of Independent Subnetwork Training

reviews.summary:
The paper provides a theoretical analysis of the convergence properties of Independent Subnetwork Training (IST), for distributed Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD) optimization with a quadratic loss function. The analysis considers both the cases of homogeneous and heterogeneous distributed scenarios, without restrictive assumptions on the gradient estimator.

The work characterizes situations where IST converges very efficiently, and cases where it does not converge to the optimal solution but to an irreducible neighbourhood. Experimental results that validate the theory are provided in the Appendix.
reviews.strengths:
The paper provides a solid analytical treatment of the important problem of distributed optimization with reduced communication overhead by means of Independent Subnetwork Training (IST).

Compared to previous work, the analysis of the paper does not rely on the restrictive assumption of a bounded stochastic gradient norm.

The paper is well written – the exposition is clear, and the material is well structured and well presented.
reviews.weaknesses:
The work considers distributed Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD) training with a quadratic loss. As mentioned by the authors in Section 3, a simple quadratic loss function has been used in other work to analyze properties of neural networks. While this loss function can still provide interesting theoretical insights, it would be valuable to extend the analysis and the experimental results to more generally used loss functions.

Minor comments:

-- Line 17: "drives from" may be changed to "derives from".

-- Equation after line 217: it seems that the first part of the equation
"$\mathbb{E}[g^k] = \bar{\mathbf{L}}^{-1} \bar{\mathbf{L}} x^k \pm \bar{\mathbf{L}}^{-1} \bar{b} - \frac{1}{\sqrt{n}} \tilde{\mathbf{D} b} = $ ..." 
should be rewritten as
"$\mathbb{E}[g^k] = \bar{\mathbf{L}}^{-1} \bar{\mathbf{L}} x^k - \frac{1}{\sqrt{n}} \tilde{\mathbf{D} b} = $ ...".

-- Equation after line 221: it seems that the first part of the equation
"$\mathbb{E}[x^{k+1}] = x^k - \gamma \mathbb{E}[g^k] = $ ..."
should be
"$\mathbb{E}[x^{k+1}] = \mathbb{E}[x^k] - \gamma \mathbb{E}[g^k] = $ ...".
reviews.questions: It would be valuable if the authors could consider a discussion or possible additional experiments to extend some of the results and insights presented in the paper to the case of distributed optimization with more generally used loss functions.
reviews.limitations: The work relates to distributed training of large-scale models, which generally correspond to significant power consumption and CO2 emissions. However, the IST method studied in the paper aims at allowing distributed training with reduced communication overhead, corresponding to potentially reduced power consumption.
reviews.rating: 7: Accept: Technically solid paper, with high impact on at least one sub-area, or moderate-to-high impact on more than one areas, with good-to-excellent evaluation, resources, reproducibility, and no unaddressed ethical considerations.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
